     2→title: "Introduction - CXO/SBU/BU/Department Head Level Leadership"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "429-500"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CXO/SBU/BU/DEPARTMENT HEAD LEVEL LEADERSHIP**
     9→
    10→**The role of Leaders in a corporate**  
    11→First and foremost, remember, Leadership does not necessarily always mean a top position like the President or Director of an organization. Even the In-Charge of the Staff in a big organization is a Leader who drives all the day-to-day staff activities. The Site Manager is also a Leader under whom various positions in the project site work for the successful completion of a project. Essentially, a Leader means someone who leads his team in all the activities that the team is responsible for.
    12→
    13→However, the weightages of top leadership is much higher since his actions will have a higher and huge impact on the organization. The weightages of the departmental heads as senior leaders is definitely less than that of the Managing Director, but nonetheless it is also very important for achievement of the goals of the organization.
    14→
    15→A Leader has multifaceted role in a corporate as narrated below:
    16→
    17→1. Visionary planning – setting a clear direction for the organization in the domain he is responsible for  
    18→2. Strategic decision – evaluating the market and developing techniques which will drive to achieve his goals  
    19→3. Corporate culture – actions in line with the cultural values of the organization  
    20→4. Stakeholder relationship – building relationship with stakeholders like employees, vendors and customers  
    21→5. Risk management – identifying potential risks and developing strategies to mitigate them  
    22→6. Leadership management – develop juniors to leaders of future   
    23→7. Finance management – monitor financial health in his domain of work
    24→
    25→Let us understand the structure of an organization and its role. An organization is usually spearheaded and driven by the Board of Directors (BoD). It forms the **Vision and Mission** of the organization and layouts a structure and foundation on which the organization treads its path to meet its goals and objectives. BoD comprises of top leaders from its own company or group companies. Now-a-days it’s mandatory to include a Director from an independent company, called the Independent Director, who would evaluate the performance of the organization from a third angle perspective. A BoD also comprises of a Lady Director who can analyse a company’s performance and activities from her perspective. The BoD may have several Directors on its Board and is led by the Chairman of the company. The Managing Director is also a part of the BoD reporting to the Chairman. The Managing Director drives the organisation in line with the Vision & Mission set by the BoD.
    26→
    27→A V**ision** statement details where the organization aspires to go. Why does your company exist? What do you hope to accomplish in the next several years? These are what a Vision addresses. But a M**ission** statement defines the **organization**’s business, its objectives, and how it will reach these objectives. The entire organization must drive their actions in line with the Vision and Mission statements of their organization.
    28→
    29→The Managing Director has under him Executive Directors and various CXOs to perform vital functions of the organisation. Each Executive Director may be working with some of the CXOs. For example, the Chief Financial Officer (CFO) looks after all the financial activities of the organisation. The Chief Strategy Officer (CSO) is responsible for finalising the strategies of different businesses of the organization, and so on. Essentially, the Managing Director and below are fully active in leading all the actions of an organization. The Chairman and the BoD may not be full time active, but reviews the organizational performance and decisions from time to time.
    30→
    31→An organisation is generally structured into different **Strategic Business Units (SBUs)** catering to different businesses of the organisation. One SBU may be involved with power projects, another SBU with Infrastructure projects like the airports, townships, etc., another SBU with Chemicals, so on and so forth. Each SBU is headed by a SBU Head who reports to a CEO (one of the CXOs). Each CXO like the CEO, CSO and CFO has a team of its own and works with each Strategic Business Unit (SBU) in that business domain. Like this, there can be several CXOs in the organisation who would report to the Executive Director or the Managing Director.
    32→
    33→Each SBU may be subdivided into different **Business Units (BUs)**. For example, the Infrastructure SBU may have a BU for airports, another BU for townships, another BU for sea-ports, etc. Each BU is headed by a BU Head and reports to the SBU Head.
    34→
    35→Each SBU may have several supporting Departments to support the different services that its BUs requires for its performance. Each such Department is driven by a Head (Department Head) who works for the BUs and may have dual reporting system, reporting to the SBU Head administratively and to the concerned CXO functionally. Examples of these support departments are Human Resources (HR), Administration, Contracts & Law, Finance & Accounts (F\&A), Audit, Information Technology (IT), Learning and Development (L\&D),  Insurance, Ethics, ESG and CSR
    36→
    37→The Chairman, Managing Director, EDs, CXOs and SBU Heads usually form the Top Management level. The BU Heads and the Departmental Heads usually form the Middle Management Levels. The Managers and Officers below the Departmental Heads usually form the Junior Management level.  
    38→The **Board of Directors (BoD)** is responsible for making key strategic decisions that determine the organization’s long-term direction, governance framework, and overall oversight.  
    39→**Top Management** translates these decisions into clear strategies and actionable plans, providing leadership, direction, and accountability across the organization.  
    40→**Middle Management** ensures coordination and alignment between strategy and operations, facilitating communication, resource deployment, and smooth execution across departments and business units.  
    41→**Junior Management** carries out the day-to-day operational activities, implementing plans, delivering results, and ensuring that organizational objectives are achieved effectively and efficiently.  
    42→To summarise, The BOD decides and frames the Vision & Mission of the organisation; The Top Management provides the **Direction** guided by the Vision and Mission of the BOD: The Middle Management is responsible for the **Facilitation** to the Junior Management for seamless performance: The Junior Management is responsible for the **Implementation** of the goals of the organisation. **Decision, Direction, Facilitation and Implementation** are the  top-down process for performance in an organisation.
    43→
    44→All references and examples of leaders in the book usually refer to these Top and Middle Management Leaders whose decisions impact the organisation significantly.
    45→
    46→**A typical Organization Structure**
    47→
    48→**TOP MANAGEMENT**  
    49→|  
    50→BOD  
    51→|  
    52→Chairman  
    53→|  
    54→Managing Director  
    55→|  
    56→Executive Director / Directors  
    57→|  
    58→CXOs  
    59→|  
    60→  SBU Heads \- AVP, VP, EVP  
    61→\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
    62→**|**  
    63→**MIDDLE MANAGEMENT**  
    64→					|			|  
    65→BU Heads 		Departmental Heads  
    66→(DGM, GM)		(AGM, Sr. Manager)  
    67→\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
    68→**|**  
    69→**JUNIOR MANAGEMENT**  
    70→|  
    71→Managers  
    72→|  
    73→Deputy Managers  
    74→|  
    75→Sr. Officers  
    76→|  
    77→Officers  
    78→\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    79→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “TOO MANY CXO” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "501-570"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “TOO MANY CXO” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Let us see how many CXOs could possibly and theoretically fit in an organisation. The Departments the CXO may head are mentioned within brackets.
    11→
    12→CAO – Chief Administrative Officer (Administration)  
    13→CBO – Chief Business Development Officer (Business Development)  
    14→CCO – Chief Customer Officer (Business Development)  
    15→CDO – Chief Design Officer (Designs & Engineering)  
    16→CEO – Chief Executive Officer (Operations)  
    17→CFO – Chief Finance Officer (Finance)  
    18→CGO – Chief Governance Officer (Audit)  
    19→CHO – Chief Human Resources Officer (HR)  
    20→CIO – Chief Information Technology Officer (Information Technology)  
    21→CJO – Chief of Joint Operations (Operations)  
    22→CKO – Chief Knowledge Officer (Learning & Development)  
    23→CLO – Chief Learning Officer (Learning & Development)  
    24→CMO – Chief Marketing Officer (Marketing)  
    25→CNO – Chief Negotiation Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
    26→COO – Chief Operating Officer (Operations)  
    27→CPO – Chief Procurement Officer (Supply Chain Management)  
    28→CQO – Chief Quality Officer (Quality)  
    29→CRO – Chief Redresser Officer (HR)  
    30→CSO – Chief Strategy Officer (Strategy)  
    31→CTO – Chief Technology Officer (Operations)  
    32→CUO – Chief Unifying Officer (HR)  
    33→CVO – Chief Vigilance Officer (Governance)  
    34→CWO – Chief Wellness Officer (HR)  
    35→CXO – Chief eXperience Officer (Strategy)  
    36→CYO – Chief Youth Officer (Administration)  
    37→CZO – Chief Zone Officer (Operations)
    38→
    39→Some names sound funny, isn’t it? That was just to have fun and complete the series from A to Z. You can also name them as you wish\! Now let us come to the main point. 
    40→
    41→Sometimes it is observed that the Top Leader tends to recruit several CXOs thinking that it will help in different activities of the organisation seamlessly and the Top Leader can be relatively free from interventions in all the activities of the Departments. However, the problems of recruiting many CXOs are as follows:
    42→
    43→1\. The Top becomes heavy and the Employee Overheads increases, as a result of which the company becomes less competitive in the market. The Top Management must be the lightest economically in an organisation. Their total salaries and number in an organisation must be restricted to avoid a Top Heavy Organisation. Their individual salaries may be high but, just to provide an idea, for a large organization, their total salaries could be around 10% of the company’s Cost to Company (CTC) and numbers restricted to around 1% of company’s total employee strength. 
    44→
    45→The Middle Level is kept heavier and the Junior Level is kept the heaviest, both in terms of total salary and numbers. Junior Level is the implementing level, hence the heaviest. However, I have observed too many CXOs being recruited by some companies making them top heavy.
    46→
    47→2\. Too many CXOs sometimes provide their own ideas which are contradicting to each other. The leaders below CXOs get confused faced with different directions from the different CXOs. For example, a CFO instructs the international Bidding team not to bid with less than 7% profit margins anywhere abroad, but the CBO asks the same team to bid with Zero profits in a particular country as entry costs to establish as a first time entrant and subsequently increase their presence. The irony is the CFO and CBO may be reluctant to discuss the issue amongst them. 
    48→
    49→3\. Often the CXOs are egoistic and some resort to politics to prove their superiority. They try to create an atmosphere down below that their directions are the most superior and need to be followed even if not suggested by other CXOs. This creates confusion down below as to whose directions need to be followed. In the above example, the CFO might try to impose his directions on the bidding team, rather than take up the matter with the CBO with the intervention of MD and form a consensus.
    50→
    51→4\. Each CXO wants to recruit and strengthen his team in the corporate office though their concerned Departments in various SBUs may have sufficient number of people of similar expertise. For example, the Finance Department of various SBUs may already have a Chartered Accountant (CA), however the CFO may intend to recruit another CA under his belt. Also these recruits, being under CXO and stationed at corporate office, try to flex their muscles to employees of the concerned Departments. This creates a non-cooperation environment between the department and the CXO’s team.
    52→
    53→**“Too many CXO”** becomes a common chant amongst employees when they find one after another CXOs being unnecessarily recruited. 
    54→
    55→The problem is that all departments need a CXO to be monitored and directed. But how many is the question. There are simple solutions to this problem. 
    56→
    57→1. Top Leaders need to combine related functions together to reduce the number of CXO recruits. With lesser number of CXOs, there will be better unity, harmony and clarity of directions in the organisation. For example, Business Development & Marketing, Advertising, Bidding & Costing and Customer Relations can be under the CBO. Finance, Accounts and Audit can be under CFO. Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness department, Unifying Officers, and Governance can be under CHRO. 
    58→
    59→2. Under each CXO, there can be different expertise in his corporate team or in the Departments to look after different activities. For example, under CHRO, there could be different Managers either in his corporate team or in the department to look after Administration, HR, Learning & Development, Wellness, and Unifying activities but CHRO remains as the overall single Chief. Also, under CFO, there could be one Chartered Accountant (CA) in his corporate team and it may not be unnecessarily duplicated by recruiting another CA in the concerned Department.  
    60→     
    61→3. Also, different CXOs should meet together to form a consensus for providing same directions to the Department or SBU Heads. For example, the CSO, CFO and the CBO must align together to decide what profit levels the Bidding team should consider to enter an international market for the first time. The profit margin to be considered could be very less if there is huge competition in that market. It could be negative, in other words, bid with anticipated loss to be sure to win, if the market is very big and the presence and learning of the market during operations is of utmost importance. It could be high if the competition is scarce and past results show that the contractors generally bid with high profit margins. There could be different scenarios, but the essence is the CSO, CBO and the CFO must form a common opinion and guide the Department in one direction. 
    62→
    63→Let me give you another example. Imagine the pandemonium that would be created if the Cricket Control Board thinks to provide best services in international cricket by recruiting a batting coach, a spin bowling coach, a fast bowling coach, a fielding coach, a wicket keeping coach, etc. Instead there can be a single coach who has a broad knowledge of all the areas of the game. In bowling itself, there are different kinds of bowlers like the off spinners, leg spinners, fast bowling, swingers, googly bowlers, etc. but that does not mean that the Board has to recruit different coaches for different kind of bowling. In batting too, some are proficient in hitting sixes, some in cover drives, and some in sweeping. All these batting abilities require different capabilities, however, irrespective of all these variations, the coach needs to be one or maximum at two who can provide all round required expertise to all the players. This would enable to provide all the players a uniform direction.
    64→
    65→Similarly pandemonium will be created if in football there are different coaches like the free kick coach, dribbling coach, penalty shootout coach, goalkeeping coach, mid fielder coach. Isn’t it?
    66→
    67→So, in an organisation, **the CXO strength must be kept to a minimum** and the various related activities of the organisation can be clubbed together under a single CXO, as illustrated above.
    68→
    69→If you become a Managing Director someday, I wish you be so, please carefully study the business model of your organization thoroughly and discuss with key people to layout your structure of CXOs. Modify them if possible if the structure does not suit with your thoughts or align with the business you are in. Provide each CXO clear roles and responsibilities having no conflict amongst each other. Organise meetings between concerned CXOs if they have different views so that a common final view can be cascaded down below to avoid confusion and provide clarity.
    70→
    71→**The salient points are:**
    72→
    73→1. Limit your number of CXO recruits  
    74→2. Limit CXO salaries as a maximum percentage of company salary  
    75→3. Engage relevant CXOs to form a consensus on strategic decisions  
    76→4. Eliminate redundancy in positions amongst Departments & CXO’s office.
    77→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “RENOWNED GUY” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "571-596"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “RENOWNED GUY” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Right person for the right job\! Readers may think what new I am saying. Yes, nothing new. Yet I cannot but state its importance again and again because I have seen organisations faltering in this area.
    11→
    12→I have observed that often very renowned personnel are recruited for important positions but eventually they have been found to be non-performers. Why? Simply because the recruit is not the expertise in the particular field even if he is renowned by name. You have to question what are you actually renowned for?
    13→
    14→For example, with due respect, Sachin Tendulkar was one of the renowned cricket players of the world but he would have probably performed badly if he was made the Hockey captain of India. Similarly Pele or Maradona were renowned footballers on earth, but they were unlikely to have performed as the Cricket captain of Brazil or Argentina. Am I correct? A Gynecologist and a Cardiologist both may have a MBBS degree and may be renowned, but you cannot expect a Gynecologist to perform a Heart Operation. Such blunders do happen in the corporate. Someone in the industry may be very renowned in Marketing but he cannot perform well if he is put into Operations. Sometimes HR gets swayed away on a person being so renowned, but the question is whether he is the right person for the right job that you are looking for?
    15→
    16→Mistakes are committed in another aspect also. Suppose you interview a Procurement officer working in a small sized SBU of a large sized company. The particular SBU may be dealing with few vendors on a small scale of annual procurements. But you get swayed away by the fact that the Procurement officer works in a very large company and thereafter provide him the position of Chief Procurement Officer in your company where you need to deal with hundreds of vendors on a large scale of annual procurements. Can he perform? Definitely not. It’s like asking the pilot of a small propeller jet to fly an A380 Airbus. Imagine the likely fate of the passengers\!
    17→
    18→Recruitment in senior positions must consider the following:
    19→
    20→1\. Understand from various sources about a new recruit on his field of expertise, the volume he deals with and the number of employees who report to him. Many candidates fake experience. A Project Manager may cite Bidding experience to try out for a senior post in Marketing. The HR must verify the credentials and actual experience. The scale of operations handled by the candidate is also to be determined. Customers and vendors may be consulted to get ideas about the candidate. Do not get swayed away just because he may be renowned in the industry, or he may be someone belonging to a large organization of repute but actually handling small scale operations.
    21→
    22→2\. HR needs to be careful about the qualifications versus experience issue. I will not prefer a highly qualified person having average skills and experience in small sized corporates; rather I would recruit a moderately qualified person having much greater experience in bigger corporates. Qualifications do count a lot. The IIT, IIM and other reputed institutional brands do count, but not at the cost of experience. I have great respect for well qualified professionals, but I have found many mediocre qualified employees performing better than them, sheerly because of better and wider experience. You may not know that most Directors of different corporates were not the best qualified engineers with the highest marks. They were definitely reasonably well qualified but with great acumen and experience to lead organisations. You may have scored the highest in your Law degree course, but you may not necessarily be the best Lawyer on earth\! Therefore while HR must consider qualifications for recruits, gauging their experience in the particular field is also extremely important.
    23→
    24→3\. Another critical point. Experience does not necessarily mean the number of years of experience after your college degrees; it is more about the talent and capability you have earned to deal with critical issues during your experience. There are middle level professionals who can deal with critical problems in a far better manner than a senior professional. Well, the question then comes, how did he at all become a senior professional? The answer could be two fold. In the earlier days, seniority was achieved by the number of years you spent after your college degrees and you may have risen based on total years of experience or you may have risen through unethical support by your unethical and biased Leaders. The point is, count on the talent and potential of the candidate rather than concentrating on his total experience. 
    25→
    26→You will find a chapter dedicated to this later titled “Grow by KNOWLEDGE, not by AGE”. Today, corporates hunt talent; they do not necessarily hunt seniority.
    27→
    28→**The salient points are:**
    29→
    30→1. Recruit right expertise for the right job, not look at qualifications alone  
    31→2. Renown does not find a place, the right expertise has  
    32→3. Total experience does not count, what counts is the talent, capability and potential
    33→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “OIL CHAIN” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "597-631"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “OIL CHAIN” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Senior Leaders must recruit other junior leaders on their merit, talent, potential and credentials. Same should be the principle while evaluating existing leaders. But sometimes senior leaders fall prey to their juniors who have expertise to find ways to make their bosses happy (this process is called oiling) through unethical means.
    11→
    12→What is oiling? Hahaha\! Everyone knows about it. But still let me try to explain it in context to corporates. Any unethical means to keep one’s boss or seniors happy is called oiling. The irony is some bosses like it. Oiling may include saying “yes sir” to everything that the boss says even if it is not right, always tells boss how great a leader he is though in reality he is not, ignores his duty and assists the boss in his personal works to please him, doing some household chores for the boss’s family, gifting the boss or his family expensive gifts, arranging parties for boss/family at his own cost, so on and so forth.
    13→
    14→An “oil chain” means a chain of leaders oiling up the ladder. Say a Department Head oils his boss who is the Business Unit Head, the Business Unit Head oils his boss who is the SBU Head, the SBU Head oils the COO, the COO oils the ED and the ED oils the MD and so on. This is an example of “Oil Chain”. The chain can be long or short depending on the chain of personalities involved. If a leader is straight forward, honest, ethical and unbiased, the oil chain stops at his subordinate level. However, this subordinate may oil the boss of his boss so that his boss gets eliminated in the chain.
    15→
    16→People are involved in the oil chain mainly to seek undue favours like promotion for himself or someone else, increments, bonus, favours in work sphere, relief from punishments in case of an offence, undue facilities like international travel, office car use, etc.  
    17→
    18→Resorting to oiling by these elements in the organization creates mistrust amongst honest employees who toil hard for the organization; they feel cheated. These honest employees may even seek to resign and try for better prospects in other good organisations. So an organisation may lose some of their talent in the process. It is said that most, may be 80%, leave organisations because of their bosses, not because of the organization itself. Therefore a boss needs to be honest and unbiased and refrain himself from being oiled.  
    19→
    20→Every leader is recruited by his higher ups at some point or the other. The HR or the senior leader in charge of such recruitments must be careful to check the **personality and sincerity of the individual**, other than his qualifications, experience, knowledge, skills and talent. Right and honest personalities keep an organisation moving from strength to strength ensuring harmony and equality amongst people. An organisation cannot afford to lose honest and talented individuals just because of their unethical bosses. Dishonest, oiling and oiled leaders cannot take an organization to great heights.
    21→
    22→Once I had an interesting discussion with one of my Executive Directors as to why many government and private organisations look into performance of individuals in Classes X and XII. What do such results mean in a corporate? The answer was very interesting.
    23→
    24→It is said that a person attains his personality traits by the age of 18\. People may be able to change their personalities thereafter but only to a certain small extent. The bulk of the characteristics of a human being are attained normally by 18 years of age. Therefore good marks scored in Classes X and XII indicate the past sincerity of the individual. If his results were good in Class X (aged approx. 16 at the time) and in class XII (aged approx. 18 at the time), it is commonly assumed that the person is sincere even now. This may not be true at all times; there are insincere people today who were sincere in their early lives and also vice-versa. However, looking into school and college results may give some idea about the sincerity of people. And it is assumed by an organisation that such sincere students would continue to work sincerely in the corporates.
    25→
    26→The moot point here is that the importance of one’s personality and sincerity of an individual cannot be over emphasised. The HR Head must observe or get feedback from deprived employees of any biased decisions taken by their leaders in the appraisal process. Oil chains significantly disturbs the appraisal process, which means, often the right candidates are not rightly rewarded. If required for senior leaders, HR can take a 360 degree feedback from those who report to the seniors with regard to their performance and appraisals. Oil chains must be eliminated to ensure that good talent prosper in the organization. Let us summarise the possible solutions;
    27→
    28→1. HR must recruit or leaders must appraise based on merit (qualifications and experience), talent, potential and credentials of employees ignoring the oil chain, if any.  
    29→     
    30→2. HR must try to ascertain the personality and character of an individual so that oil chains do not flourish.  
    31→3. HR may keep a good watch during appraisal process where the oiled leaders tend to give good appraisals to underserving employees.
    32→
    33→4. HR must also note the achievement of individuals from time to time so that they can also contribute positively during the appraisal process.  
    34→     
    35→
    36→**The salient points are:**
    37→
    38→1. Appraise based on merit, talent, potential and credentials  
    39→2. Discourage oiling and break oil chains  
    40→3. Personality, honesty and sincerity of an individual matters, not only his qualifications, experience, knowledge, skills and talent  
    41→4. If bosses display true unbiased leadership, talent can be retained
    42→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “LETHARGIC AND REACTIVE” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "632-657"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “LETHARGIC AND REACTIVE” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Have you read the book “The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People”. The first chapter itself deals with the habit of being proactive. 
    11→
    12→Please understand that some of your competitors, may not be all, are always on the lookout for opportunities. So when an organisation comes across an opportunity, it should be quick to act upon. Or if the CSO has come up with a good Strategy, the organization must be quick to implement the same. 
    13→
    14→Once at a point of time, an organization had ample opportunity to bag construction projects in an African country. But there were lot to study and understand to avail the opportunities like the labour and tax laws of the country, the infrastructure available, the anticipated profit levels, the vendors and subcontractors available, the award system, the repatriation laws, the organization’s manpower required in the country, the office location, overall costs, etc.
    15→
    16→Every analysis for the above started but very slowly. It was more than a year that above analysis was completed. By the time, one competitor did their due diligence fast and was quick to secure an order too. This organization was late. When an organization enters a new market for the first time with the presence of only a few competitors, you can expect good profit margins. But as time goes by, more competitors come in, competition increases, chances to win decreases, and profit margins dwindles even if you win. 
    17→
    18→In the life time of a particular business, whether the business is related to a product or a service or a project where products and services are integrated, you will find that the profit margins during the early period of the lifetime is high and thereafter it diminishes to a point when running the business makes no sense. That is why you need to attempt and grab opportunities at the earliest. If the opportunities require lot of due diligence before you dive into those, you need to study and complete your due diligence and take necessary actions very fast so that you can thereafter try to avail of those opportunities. The sooner, the better, is the mantra. 
    19→
    20→Also an organization must be innovative to produce new products or a service or find new methods and new technologies to implement a project. However, even a new product or service will have a lifetime with the phenomenon of diminishing returns over a period of time. So the constant endeavor is to constantly change with constantly high speed. It could be a change in the market, change in technology, change in the product or service, etc. Constant change and upgrading an organization is the key to success but such changes have to be fast in order to grab the opportunities with optimum profits.
    21→
    22→In another example, an organization had lot of business opportunities in some countries. But the problem was the prices to be quoted were fixed. The projects involved many raw materials for supplies where there were fluctuations in the cost of raw materials over time. Also the bid prices to be quoted were in dollars whereas the supporting vendors quoting to the organization were in INR (Indian Rupees). So there was currency fluctuation risk too involved in the bid price. To be safe from such fluctuations, the organization knew that there were hedging processes available in banks which charged a premium for the coverage. But the organization did not know the entire process of hedging and the cost thereof. Studies started but at a very slow pace. During this time, the competitors, who were already experienced in the hedging process, grabbed lot of opportunities with good profits.
    23→
    24→Therefore the faster you grab an opportunity, the better returns and also the number of opportunities you get. You must be proactive all the time. That’s the essence. Don’t be lethargic and reactive and then go to a saturated market after your competitors have grabbed the initial opportunities. The CSO, CBO and the SBU Head must act very fast and request other CXO or Department Heads to be proactive to learn the required new aspects of an opportunistic market and dive into the market as soon as possible to reap the maximum benefits. 
    25→
    26→**The salient points are:**
    27→
    28→1. Be proactive to learn very fast against first time challenges  
    29→2. Grab opportunities at the earliest  
    30→3. Reap benefits during initial phases of business cycle  
    31→4. Continuously change and innovate to stay relevant  
    32→5. Leave the market, if need be, after it is saturated with undesirable margins
    33→

     2→title: "THE “SHOOTING BELOW” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "658-685"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “SHOOTING BELOW” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→This is one of the unwanted consequences of the Oil Chain Syndrome described earlier. Say, a Managing Director (MD) wants to give a stern warning to his subordinate CXO for some lapses but the CXO oils the MD at all times. In this situation, the MD is hesitant and lacks courage to go for direct confrontation with the CXO. Instead the MD rebukes the subordinate of the COO, say the SBU Head. In this manner, the MD tries to warn the CXO through the SBU Head in the presence of CXO. The CXO knows that he is actually accountable for the lapses in question, yet he keeps mum or even supports the MD while the SBU Head is rebuked for no fault of his. The CXO can politely accept the lapses and defend the SBU Head. However, the CXO is afraid to do so because he does not want to ruin his prospects of promotion, increment and bonus in the upcoming appraisal by angering the MD and not taking his side. The SBU Head simply becomes a scape goat and an indirect medium to convey warnings to his CXO.
    11→
    12→The personality and sincerity of the leader then comes into question. Until someone is not responsible for lapses, he does not deserve to become a scape goat. In such times of crisis, a leader must support his subordinate. In the above case the CXO should have defended the SBU Head. I am not saying he should have argued and fought with the MD, but he could have been tactful to agree and take responsibility to what the MD said and take necessary steps to avoid such lapses in future. The CXO should defend the SBU Head and submit that he with the support of the SBU Head would jointly take care of the concerns raised by the MD in future. Personality, sincerity and ownership of lapses are traits of good leadership which must be demonstrated at such times. Accepting a mistake or a lapse by a leader is not demining him. A leader executes thousands of actions in his lifetime and some mistakes are bound to happen sometimes. Owning responsibility and defending subordinates are great attributes of a good leader. 
    13→
    14→If such incidences prolong, the SBU Head may lose his enthusiasm and trust towards his seniors. He may gang up with other like-minded colleagues and create an atmosphere of enmity against such seniors. 
    15→
    16→Senior leaders must demonstrate courage to accept responsibilities for their own faults and lapses and insulate his juniors from being blasted. Even in case a junior makes unintended mistakes, he should be shielded to the maximum possible extent. Such leadership builds immense unity in the team and strengthens the bonds between the team members which eventually lead to build the organization to greater heights. In an organization, utmost care should be taken so that no leader demines the dignity and ego of any other leader, senior or junior. 
    17→
    18→**Shooting the messenger does no good to an organization.**
    19→
    20→When a leader owns a lapse or a mistake and shields his subordinate, the following things are likely to happen.
    21→
    22→1. The boss of the leader forms a high opinion on the personality of the leader. The leader himself tries to find out the solution to the mistake he made. The subordinates too hail the leader of such traits.  
    23→     
    24→2. The subordinates’ gets courage to work freely and not worry about mistakes since they know their leader is there to take care of them in times of unintentionally made mistakes or unavoidable crisis.  
    25→     
    26→3. Immense trust develops amongst subordinates and the team works unitedly leading to greater achievements.  
    27→     
    28→4. Subordinates feel the joy of work. They work with passion and not with stress.
    29→
    30→**The salient points are:**
    31→
    32→1. Deal with leaders directly for mistakes, don’t bring in someone as a scape goat to act as a messenger  
    33→2. Defend subordinates when they have done nothing wrong or tried at something but unsuccessful  
    34→3. It requires courage to own mistakes and responsibility, but that’s true leadership
    35→

     2→title: "THE “PASSING INCOMPETENCE” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "686-704"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “PASSING INCOMPETENCE” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→It is obvious that a leader cannot have expertise in all areas of a business domain. Therefore, down below, other leaders are recruited who can fill up these gaps. For example, a Director may not have immense knowledge on Finance, but below him the CFO has. Therefore different levels of leaders complement each other to complete expertise in all the fields.
    11→
    12→Sometimes there are situations when none of the leaders, at whatsoever level, can achieve their goals at desired times. For example, a client was under financial stress and could not release payments to a company at stipulated times as per the contract. There were considerable delays in receiving these payments and the client repeatedly maintained that it will take some time to make the payments. However, during reviews with the Director and COO of the company, the SBU Head was blasted for not being able to get the payments from the clients even after citing reasons of the client and the extreme efforts that the SBU was taking in following up for the payments. 
    13→
    14→Once a SBU Head informed his Director that since he has not been able to get the payments on time he could arrange a meeting with the MD of the client with his Director so that the Director himself could pursue the client to release the payments. To this suggestion, the Director was afraid who knew within himself that given the circumstances he too would not be able to realize the payments even if he met the MD of the client. But he conveniently passes the buck to the SBU Head knowing it fully well that he himself could not have received payment from the client given the circumstances the client was in. This is called “passing incompetence”, in other words, blaming down below even when the leader himself is incompetent for the job in his own field of expertise.
    15→
    16→When a leader himself is not competent to achieve a goal due to some specific constraints, he should not blast his subordinates for not being able to achieve the same. Rather in such circumstances, the leader should be considerate and discuss with his subordinate on the ways and means to deal with the situation. If the leader thinks there could be a way out to receive the payments with his support, he can guide the SBU Head accordingly. Passing incompetence is just a blame game; it does no good to an organization.  
    17→
    18→In another instance, it was revealed that a Senior Design Engineer could not optimise his designs of structures in a bid submitted to an African client. It was found that there was a lack of knowledge on some of the parameters of the design codes pertaining to the country. The bid submitted was not at all competitive. The Design Head blasted the Senior Design Engineer for his failure though it was he himself who had approved the designs for submission in the bid. The Design Head himself was not aware of the Design codes of that country. With his own lack of knowledge, it was not right for him to blast the Senior Design Engineer for such lapses. Instead they could have discussed jointly on how could they handle the issue in the future bids.  
    19→
    20→Unnecessarily blasting juniors in times of crisis when top leaders themselves do not have solutions to a problem leads to nowhere, in fact, it breaks the morale and motivation of his people. All leaders, seniors and juniors, should work together to find solutions in times of crisis. **There is no point in passing your incompetence to your juniors.** 
    21→
    22→**The salient points are:**
    23→
    24→1. There is nothing wrong to be incompetent in a subject. But rebuking subordinate for incompetency is shameful when the leader himself lacks competency  
    25→2. Mitigation measures need to discuss by bosses and subordinates and work together in subjects where there is incompetency and lack of knowledge.
    26→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “PAST PERFORMANCE” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "705-731"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “PAST PERFORMANCE” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Some Leaders may have done exceptionally well in the past. A Head talked about great innovations made during execution of many tough projects in the past and spent hours detailing them in all possible meetings and gatherings. Slowly, people started feeling bored listening to the same story again and again. They made fun of the leader outside the company premises, of course not in presence of the leader out of fear of being victimized later. 
    11→
    12→You may have seen the blockbusters “Sholay” or the “Titanic” once or twice or may be a few times more but definitely not 100 times. Even if you like watching movies, you will want to watch other new movies. Isn’t it? Will you not get bored by watching a movie again and again after a point of time\!
    13→
    14→Forcefully parroting lessons by leaders to juniors who have already learned about the same earlier creates boredom and frustration. It’s great to know how leaders have handled crisis in the past. But people don’t like listening to the story repeatedly again and again. People want to hear new stories of modern day challenges which will create interest, inspiration, passion and enthusiasm to others. People today don’t want to hear on how type writers were manufactured and used which they might already know; rather they would wish to learn about modern topics like how Artificial Intelligence impacts our industry and society at large. And after they learn about this, they will wish to learn newer things that are happening on this earth.
    15→
    16→We all know performance on time with Key Result Areas (KRAs) is the basis of growth and sustainability of an organisation. An organisation may have given you credit and rewards for your past performance, but that is past and you can bask in glory at most may be for a year or more. But later on, the organisation is not interested of your achievements of the past. However talented you are or claim to be so, the organisation looks upon you to tide over the current challenges and perform in the current projects. If you cannot perform today, you do not deserve any rewards irrespective of many that you may have received in the past. You can use your learning’s of the **past**, but perform in the **present**, and secure the organisation for the **future**.
    17→
    18→You have seen many famous sportsmen and women hanging their boots at a particular stage of their career. Why? Should they continue because their past performance has created world records? Not at all. They can continue as long as they can perform. Same is the case with employees in organisation. If you do not hang your boots on continuous nonperformance, the company will arrange to hang those for you\!
    19→
    20→**The only “constant” on this earth is “change”**. Everything is changing with the passage of time, that too at a fast pace. New machineries and equipment are discovered which changes the way you work, clients adopt to new methodologies, gadgets change, financing mechanisms change, strategies change, the laws change, and most importantly people too change. With so much of changed environment, people need to adapt to the current environment, learn and implement new things and keep on innovating so that the change remains eternal. This is how you can make your organisation stand up against the crowd and lead as the market leader in the world faced with stiff competition everywhere.
    21→
    22→Repeated narratives about past performance are the syndrome we are discussing about. One needs to get rid of this habit and come back to “present sense”. New things, new innovations, new challenges, new ways to perform is what people are interested to learn. If you have reached the “max cap” (please see Annexure discussing on “Max cap”), be humble to quit and allow your capable juniors to assume your place and perform.
    23→
    24→The HR must keep its eyes and ears open on past stories of leaders. You can assume that if the same stories continue, then the leader has nothing new to say. It’s a point where you need to intervene and assess the future capability of the leader to take ahead the organization in the future. The future of an organization is definitely weak with leaders suffering from this syndrome.
    25→
    26→The HR Head should not be influenced and carried away with the magnificent stories of the past performance of a leader. **Performance Appraisals are for current year ONLY, not for the past. And Potential Appraisals too are for the future ONLY, not for the past.**
    27→
    28→**The salient points are:**
    29→
    30→1. Past performance is history, current performance is what matters in appraisals and potential performance is what the leader would take the organization ahead in the future  
    31→2. Teach new things, people will listen and learn with interest  
    32→3. The only “constant’ on earth is “change”; learn and implement such changes  
    33→4. Change to stay relevant and secure your future and your organisation
    34→

     2→title: "THE “EGO” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "732-759"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “EGO” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→A Leader possesses great leadership qualities to sustain and grow an organisation but he is unlikely to have expertise in all the fields of expertise like Marketing, HR, Finance, Project Management (PM), Contracts, Law, Costing, Supply Chain Management (SCM), Quality, Safety, etc.
    11→
    12→Say, for example, a SBU Head may be jealous or angry with Contracts Head for various reasons, personal or official. It could be because of the Contacts Head being known as a super performer, or that the Contracts Head does not oil the SBU Head, or because the Contracts Head owns a Mercedes which the SBU Head does not, or could be that the Contracts Head is acknowledged and always appreciated by the SBU Head’s boss. It could be for so many reasons.  
    13→
    14→Now, suppose some critical discussions are required for a client’s meeting pertaining to Contracts clauses. The SBU Head, out of jealousy and anger, plans to take the Project Management (PM) Head along with him assuming that the PM can handle issues pertaining to Contracts. Say, the SBU Head & PM Head signs off some agreements with the client after the discussions. It may be noted that even if the PM is generally aware of Contracts clauses he does not have the expertise in the field and may not be able to read in between the lines. Later on it is found that the organisation is in deep trouble because of certain terms signed off in the agreement with the client which the signatories, i.e. the SBU Head and the PM Head were not capable to gauge its impact.
    15→
    16→Let me give an example. A particular clause in the Agreement read that the contractor shall be liable for any consequential damages for not completing the project on stipulated time. Neither the SBU Head nor the PM Head could understand the significance of this clause; however, they signed the agreement with the client. They were confident to complete the project on time, hence that clause did not mean much to them. They just signed it off. This clause is a dangerous clause in the Contracts parlance. This means, if the project is not executed on time, the contractor shall have to pay for all the liabilities of the client with its purchasers, which may run into millions of dollars.
    17→
    18→Let me explain. Say, the client has entered to an agreement with an Electricity Board for supplying a committed quantum of electricity every month after the scheduled completion of a Power Transmission Project. Say, there is a stipulated amount of penalty per month of delay to be levied by the Electricity Board to the client for failing to do so because in such an event the Electricity Board cannot supply power to its retail customers. Say, the project is being executed by its contractor awarded by the client and the contractor has signed the contractual clause on consequential damage with the client. Now, in case the contractor fails to complete the Power Transmission Line on time, it has to pay up all the penalties that the Electricity Board would levy on the client for its delay to sell power to the Board. This is what is called “Consequential Damages”. Such penalties may be huge, even more than the revenues earned by the contractor during the execution of the project.
    19→
    20→What is the lesson? Never try to act wise on any field where you do not have full expertise. The SBU Head should not have been egoistic and should have taken the Contracts Head along with him irrespective of his bitter relationship, at least for the sake of the organisation. Jealousy and ego have no place in company dealings. After all it’s a team work to do the best for the company.
    21→
    22→In the above example of consequential damages, if an organization has to pay a huge sum to the client on this account, it may severely impact the financial results of the organization. This may affect the pay, bonus and increments of the employees in the next financial year. It may lose its standing amongst its competitors. Therefore the impact could be wide enough impacting the business and people. 
    23→
    24→If you have a stomach upset, your nearby residing doctors like the eye specialist or the Gynecologist too can advise you in general. After all, they are basically doctors with MBBS degrees. But if the stomach upset is critical and is just a symptom of a greater disorder in the body, you must go to a Doctor of Medicine or an Endocrinologist who have better expertise of evaluating such stomach pains. The eye specialist or the Gynecologist may not have the expertise for dealing with such critical disorders. Same is the case in corporates. The SBU Head, BU Head, Contracts Head, SCM Head, Marketing Head, Project Management Head, etc. may all be well qualified engineers but they have different level of expertise in different domains.  
    25→
    26→In the interest of the organisation, any leader must take the advantage of the best expertise available in his team keeping aside personal differences. If the company does well, all leaders will benefit otherwise all will suffer. HR must be vigilant to come across existence of such bitter relationships between leaders. They can organise business games, get together and sports to foster team work, harmony and a sense of oneness and belongingness amongst employees.
    27→
    28→**The salient points are:**
    29→
    30→1. Ego has no place in business, consult the expertise without ego  
    31→2. Don’t act wise in a subject with lack of expertise  
    32→3. A leader have no right to be egoistic and fail an organization  
    33→4. Ego, jealousy and selfishness are signs of failing leadership  
    34→5. HR may organise different events to foster harmony and belongingness
    35→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “BLUE-EYED BLIND-EYED” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "760-791"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “BLUE-EYED BLIND-EYED” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Sometimes a Top Leader or a Head is too biased and very fond of some individuals, so much so that he ignores the wrong doings of these individuals. It could be primarily for two reasons. One, you don’t have the capability to see what wrongs are being done by these people; and two, you are turning a blind eye on them because they oil you. You must have already read the chapter on the “Oil Chain” Syndrome where such individuals play a vital part in the chain. 
    11→
    12→In one instance, one Departmental Head was misusing the car and driver allotted to the company. By virtue of his closeness with his Leader, he considered it as his right to use the car for personal travel on weekends. Both the Administration Head and the SBU Head turned a blind eye because the Department Head and the Administration Head used to oil the SBU Head. The Administration or any other employee for that matter was fearful to report such activities lest they may get victimized. That is why all leaders must display strong personality and courage to stop such undesired activities. There were forums to secretly and anonymously report such misuse but still employees were fearful lest their names become known somehow at a later stage.
    13→
    14→Another Head was running a subcontractor company of his own with owners as his relatives. The subcontractor was receiving orders from the approval of the Head itself. The CXO turned a blind eye to what the Head was doing unethically, surely for vested interests.
    15→
    16→Another Head was always promoting a particular individual every two years with a performance level which was attained by many other individuals. In other words, that particular individual was given special preference for promotions while others with the same performance levels were ignored. Such was the biasedness of the Head.
    17→
    18→These individuals who take unethical advantages from their leaders are what I call the “Blue-eyed” people and their leaders the “blind-Eyed” leaders. This is the “Blue-eyed Blind-eyed” syndrome. You must be equal with all your employees. You cannot close your eyes for people who oil you. You must be vigilant all round, even with high level performers. When you trust people with eyes closed, sometimes certain individuals’ wrong the company for which the organization suffers. Sometimes you as a leader may also not know how people down below whom you always trusted are committing wrong doings. You must keep your eyes open at all times.
    19→
    20→Administration Head should be able to identify such wrong doings of individuals and also identify leaders who purposefully provide undue advantage to these individuals. If a Head is afraid lest he be victimized, then I would say that the Head is not fit for his job. At least he can inform about the wrong doings to his superior. In fact, Administration should be strong enough in an organisation so that they can ensure that the individuals and leaders are disciplined and obey the rules of the organization. They can take people to task who do not fall in line. After all, discipline must prevail in an organization. 
    21→
    22→In fact, it is more dangerous if a blue eyed person is actually incompetent. Such incompetence will bring down the strength of an organization and also bring down the morale of competent people.
    23→
    24→Regardless of position, any Head as a leader must act on wrong doings. That is how an organisation earns its reputation for equality to all employees and thereby the employees try to give their best for the company. Because they would always then know that the best will be rewarded and their endeavor will not go to waste. 
    25→
    26→I have seen many “blue eyed” employees securing good positions in organization who did not deserve to get such positions but succeeded because of their “blind eyed” leaders. 
    27→
    28→Over the years an organization may have many positions occupied by undeserving employees. So, many corporates undertake the exercise of “Level” interviews. These interviews are held on a one to one basis basically to determine the experience, talent and potential required of a particular position / Level in a company vis-à-vis what experience, talent and potential the employee occupying the position have. If there is a mismatch, he could be demoted or even promoted if the Level interview finds him fit for a higher position. If there is a match in the Level interview, then he could carry on with the same position he is in. In this way, an organization can restructure and underserving employees occupying different positions can be asked to occupy higher or lower position / Level as deemed fit by the Level interview.
    29→
    30→It is a matter of shame and loss of prestige if one has to be demoted after a Level interview. However many organisations may not prefer to demote employees for adverse reactions, however, the organization may at least be careful not to promote such employees in near future until they attain the potential required by higher positions.
    31→
    32→**The salient points are:**
    33→
    34→1. Don’t be blind eyed even to blue eyed employees  
    35→2. Leaders have to keep their eyes and ears opened to see and hear wrong things happening around  
    36→3. Blue eyed employees may be unfortunately be incompetent or occupying hot seats which they do not deserve  
    37→4. Level interviews may be conducted after every three or five years to restructure people in appropriate positions  
    38→5. Level interviews are very sensitive because demotions or promotions may take place. HR needs to sensitise its employees about the process.
    39→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “ALL DAY VC” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "792-821"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “ALL DAY VC” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→“Meetings, meetings everywhere, nor a time to work”. It is like “Water, water everywhere, nor a drop to drink”. Meetings are an essential part of one’s work. Through meetings, we determine our course of action. But sometimes what happens is ridiculous. The top leaders, without discussing amongst each other, keep meetings with a SBU or a Department one after another, and thereby the employees of the Department have no time to work except to attend meetings.
    11→
    12→Let me give an example. The MD calls for a review meeting with a particular SBU at 10 am in the morning for one hour. The CSO calls for a meeting with the same SBU on important strategic matters at 11.15 am for half an hour. The CPO calls for a meeting with the SBU at 12 noon for one hour. After lunch time, the CFO calls for a meeting with the SBU at 2 pm for two hours. This goes on till the end of the day. When will the SBU employees work? Senior leaders must discuss with each other and schedule their weekly or monthly meetings in advance and ensure that a SBU is not held back continuously for meetings.
    13→
    14→Also, it is noticed that after VC meetings have become popular, the meetings are often unnecessarily lengthy. They are not to the point; at least in the beginning, and two, they happen very frequently as compared to the time when physical meetings used to happen. Seniors are supposedly intelligent enough to grasp the essence of data and information and subsequent actions to be taken discussed in a meeting. Just because VC is available at your fingertips, you cannot hold too many meetings unnecessarily and hold the SBU or a Department at ransom.
    15→
    16→The solution to this syndrome is to keep senior leadership meetings only once a month, unless anything urgent crops up. They should be well structured, short and to the point. The tasks to do should be SMART, i.e. Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Result oriented and Time bound. The Head of the organisation should organise the overall meetings of all the departments at one go once a month to ensure that seniors have sufficient time to think on that and work.
    17→
    18→In an era of technology, Heads resort to VC meetings. Yes, it provides connect at ease in the comfort of your own office and also saves cost against physical presence for meetings. However, leaders too are human beings with sentiments and emotions. The emotional connect does not happen in VC meetings. It’s like watching a match at home with some family members versus watching a match in the stadium with a packed yelling crowd. The emotions and enthusiasm in a stadium is entirely different. Emotions connect people and their thoughts and resolve through physical meetings are very strong and effective. Mental connect is essential to boost effectiveness of meetings.
    19→
    20→In fact, before the VC era, physical meetings and over the phone meetings used to take place for reviews. After the ‘VC” stepped in, Leaders and Heads developed an inclination to hold VC meetings even for trivial matters. The number of meetings happening now is far higher than the era before, yet performance levels were presumably the same. More VC meetings do not necessarily mean better performance of the organisation. For some leaders, it has become a status show and an unnecessary habit.
    21→
    22→The solution to this is simple. Keep a face to face meeting once in a quarter, rest may be on VCs on a structured scheduled plan. This will ensure not only your comfort to conduct VC meetings and save cost, but also add that emotional connect with your employees to boost confidence and effectiveness. 
    23→
    24→Once in a quarter, even if not required, I used to go for a visit to my clients. They would first enquire about the work that I have come for, and I used to say I have come only for a courtesy meeting over a cup of tea and I have no agenda. You cannot believe how delighted they used to be. Such personal connect enhances the emotional index between a contractor and a client fostering a good understanding partnership to achieve common business goals.
    25→
    26→If your child loves you but stays away for studies or work, both of you get connected over mobile phones and converse as you wish. You may be talking to your child many times a month. But when he comes to meet you in person on a holiday and gives you a hug, it says a lot more than thousands of minutes you may have spoken to him on your mobile. This is what is emotional connect. Similar will be the case with your client, not for expecting undue favours, but for developing a good professional and a business relationship.
    27→
    28→Keep VC meetings only when absolutely necessary. Trivial matters can be discussed over phone and emails. Your status as a Head cannot be enhanced with your show off on VC meetings; in fact, all employees down to the staff level are intelligent enough to know your track record and performance. They will not be swayed away if you keep multiple VC meetings. You cannot keep your performance and credibility a secret. After all, at the end of the year, no one will question on the number of VC meetings you had, everyone will question on the performance you displayed vis-à-vis the KRAs which was set up for you at the beginning of the year.
    29→
    30→**The salient points are:**
    31→
    32→1. Meetings must be organized periodically, but discussions should be to the point  
    33→2. VC meetings saves time and money, but don’t incline to have too many VC meetings, otherwise employees will not have much time to work   
    34→3. CXOs must sit together to set meetings for a SBU over the month. In the process, all the CXOs will be aware of the monthly schedule of their meetings with the SBU  
    35→4. Physical meetings connect people emotionally, VC meetings cannot. Organise physical meetings at least once in a quarter.  
    36→5. Developing emotional connect through physical meetings with a client is not for seeking undue favours but for a better professional business relationship.
    37→

     2→title: "THE “NO EMERGENCY” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "822-855"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “NO EMERGENCY” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→“Time and Tide waits for none”. We are all aware of this famous phrase. You can purchase anything on earth which you have lost. Whether you lose your mobile or a watch or a laptop or office equipment or construction machinery or whatever, you can spend money and purchase it once again. Can you purchase time which is lost? Yesterday is gone, but today is what you are in. Time gone away is gone forever. No amount of money or magic can bring back your lost time.
    11→
    12→Be it for your personal or social life or corporate life, time is one of the precious assets that the Almighty has given to you to utilize it in the best possible manner. It’s limited. It’s only 24 hours a day and till what age of your life that is uncertain. Optimum usage of this time should be of immense importance to an individual.
    13→
    14→The CEO/ Business Unit Head must give appropriate time to all its Units/Departments as and when necessary. **Often this is ignored and more interaction time is given to Business Units of his likings or expertise**. It is obvious that a Leader cannot have interest in all business areas or in all different Departments, yet he remains responsible for their performance. The CEO/Head must provide his precious time to all Business Units /Departments every month so that all the Business Units/Department feels important and gain from the precious advises and suggestions they receive in such meetings. It acts as a big boost to the performance of the Units/Departments with the support received from the CXO/Heads.
    15→
    16→Sometimes, CXOs pay more attention to performing Units so that the Units can perform better and enhance the performance of the organization as a whole. This could also happen because the CXO has more interest or expertise in the business domain of the Unit. In this scenario, the Units which are performing inferiorly are let down. These Units do not receive proper advice to grow and are therefore left behind. 
    17→
    18→Vice-versa, the CXO may pay more attention to Units with relatively poor performance because they need more attention. In this scenario, the performing Units think that they could have further excelled had the CXO given more time to them. 
    19→
    20→It is often said, **“Build on your strength, and work on your weaknesses”**. This means that senior leaders need to pay appropriate attention to performing Units as well as to Units with relatively lesser performance. Every individual or every organisation cannot be a super expert in all fields of action. You may have strengths in some, weakness in some. The more strength you add to a performing Unit and the more weakness you reduce in a non performing Unit build an organization from strength to strength. Time is precious, so you need to balance and organise your time accordingly with all your Business Units and Departments. Employees may require time from their CXO/Heads for discussion on important matters or approach the HR in case they cannot make direct contacts with their senior leaders.
    21→
    22→Similarly, the CXO/Heads must spend some time for its clients, vendors and partners. In fact Business Development is one of the key aspects of a CXO and some Heads. This enhances understanding and cooperation between the client/vendor and the customer and both the “Partners in progress” feels each other’s importance and necessity to successfully implement projects. Therefore, occasional meetings with such stake holders are also similarly important. Don’t ignore them.
    23→
    24→HR can organise Employee Satisfaction Survey (ESS), Customer Satisfaction Survey (CSS) and Vendor Satisfaction Survey (VSS) with appropriate questionnaire to understand how the different stake holders think about the Leadership of the organisation. Based on these feedbacks, HR and the senior leaders can take initiatives to improve business processes as found appropriate from the surveys.  
    25→   
    26→Now let us talk about Emergency situations. We have all heard about 24/7 Emergency wards in hospitals. Of course corporates do not encounter such situations. But sometimes, an emergency situation do arise in corporates which requires immediate and special approval of the management since the requirements may be beyond the Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) laid down by the company. 
    27→
    28→Examples of such situations could be an immediate action on a project so that the client does not impose Liquidated Damages, or an immediate vendor payment required so that the vendor does not divert a critical equipment to another contractor, or an immediate recruitment of a Safety Officer in a dangerous project site or hospitalising an employee urgently with required admission charges to save his life or an immediate approval of discount for an ongoing reverse auction of a bid. These situations warrant immediate and special approvals from the appropriate approval authority who may have to leave their current work or meeting and urgently attend to the issue. Sometimes such special approvals may be required even after office hours.   
    29→In some organization, I came to know about a Leader who was not available when an urgent permission was required to pass on more discounts in an ongoing online reverse auction of a bid. Acquiring the business was very important but the Leader was busy in a meeting. What happened then was that the Bidding Head could not pass on possible discounts and the prospective bid was lost. Is a meeting more important than foregoing a possibility to win a project? Could be, depending on the importance of the meeting. When a project is won, it’s executed and only then the profits are realized which help the organization to distribute salaries to all its employees. So, was not time to be given in such emergency situations for winning a project more important? When two or more important things to be done crop up at the same time, a leader needs to weigh the importance of each and attend those one by one based on priorities set. A leader should not ignore an event just because it was not scheduled before, he has to attend based on emergency, priority and the impact it would have in the organization. 
    30→
    31→The top Leader is usually busy with his appointments but sometimes fails to recognise such emergencies. I have seen projects getting stalled for defaults of vendor payments and thereafter deliveries diverted to other competitors, which could have been avoided with the immediate intervention of senior leaders on an emergency basis. Yet the Top Leadership expects projects to be completed on time even if they fail to provide time and approvals in urgent emergency situations.
    32→
    33→Untimely action and not attending to emergency situations often proves fatal or detrimental to progress of projects and order book. The Top Leader must set aside his important meetings and attend to emergency situations first. He must understand that **“Emergency intervention comes first, scheduled meetings later”.**
    34→
    35→**The salient points are:**
    36→
    37→1. Time is precious. One cannot get back lost time  
    38→2. Provide appropriate time to all BUs irrespective of your inclination and expertise  
    39→3. Leave scheduled appointments and attend to Emergency situations  
    40→4. Deciding in emergency situations can save an organization of dangers or bring immediate opportunities to organisations
    41→
