     2→title: "THE “NO RISK” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "856-887"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “NO RISK” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→**“No risk, no reward” or “No risk, no game”**. You must have heard these phrases. Even with good capability and talent of employees, sometimes the Head lacks confidence and is avert to taking risks. You cannot expect rewards without taking risks. Risks are of two types – blind risks and calculated risks. Blind risks are like lottery, there is no credit to winning or losing the lottery, it’s sheer luck. But calculated risks are based on evaluating the risk factors and their likelihood to occur (probability). The product of the risk factor and the likelihood (i.e. probability) is the risk coefficient. For example, an occurrence of a severe earthquake is a high risk factor. But Karnataka being in Seismic Zone 2 has a very less probability of an earthquake happening. So the risk coefficient of a house in Karnataka facing earthquake is very small. Similarly Lung Cancer is a high risk critical ailment, but probability of the ailment in a health conscious nonsmoker and a non-drinker individual living in a clean non polluted environment is very less. Therefore the risk coefficient of lung cancer in such an individual is very less.
    11→
    12→Now, here is an example on a business opportunity. A Business Unit, with good performance in project execution in India and in some countries, intends to commence business in a new continent. Say, the cost of Marketing in a year in the new country would be around USD 0.25 Million. The Business Unit, after studying in details about the opportunities, bidding and execution process, laws of the country, the competition, the profit levels, etc. expects winning at least one USD 25 Million job (of 2 years duration) with 5% net profit. Marketing costs at the end of 3 years (one year to grab first order & two years to execute) would be USD 0.75 Million (USD 0.25 Million x 3 years) whereas the net profit generated at the end of three years would be USD 1.125 Million (5% of USD 25 Million). You can recover your costs with one project itself in three years’ time. But then you need to take such calculated risk and invest for the Marketing costs.
    13→
    14→The risk factor of not securing a project is very high but in the above scenario of possessing good experience, the probability of not securing a project is very less. Therefore, the risk coefficient, i.e. product of the risk factor and the probability, is very less.
    15→
    16→After weighing the risk factors and their probabilities and drawing the risk-probability matrix, the Head can take risk to go ahead and establish an office for future business in a foreign continent. If you lack confidence and take no risk, you are safe but will fail to grow your organisation.  
    17→In any business opportunity, you will come across risks. The way to go about is like this. Firstly, please note down the risks of the factors and their corresponding probabilities of likeliness to happen. Multiply the risk factor with the probability resulting in the risk coefficient. If the risk coefficient is high in most of the risk factors, then the business opportunity can be classified as very risky, else it could be non-risky.
    18→
    19→The appetite to take risks too varies from organization to organization. If my net worth is only Rs 10 Lakhs, can I take the risk of a Rs 2 Lakh lottery ticket even if the prize money is Rs 10 Crores. No, because the risk of losing the money is very high compared to the net worth I have. So risk taking capability of organization too varies.
    20→
    21→The risk-probability matrix has to be analysed in details by leaders and at a fast pace, so as not to give enough time to other competitors to grab the opportunities. The matrix needs to analysed fast and a decision has to be taken fast on whether the organization would like to go ahead with the evaluated risk coefficient.
    22→
    23→An important note on evaluating risks. In any option in life including in the above example of setting up a new office in a new continent involving capital costs, there will be many pros and cons. In the above the case, the pros factors could be entrance to a new market, expansion of business, having new clients, diversification of business, additional manpower engagement, etc. The cons could be the costs involved, new domain of business which will demand new learning, adapting to new business environment, etc.  
    24→
    25→Now the point is, very unfortunately, there are some leaders who would at once jump upon the negative points of an option and discourage from going ahead in such options out of fear because of the risks involved. On the contrary, there are leaders who would weigh the impact of the positive points of an option, and if found to be attractive, would try to find out mitigation measures for the negative points the option may have. These leaders would definitely be successful who are positive in their attitude, can weigh the positive sides of an option and not ignore but find mitigation measures to counter the negative points the option may have. Leaders who are not calculated risk driven can never rise in life and can never grow an organisation. 
    26→
    27→However, this is not to say that you will succeed in all options wherever you have taken calculated risks. Decisions taken do fail you sometimes, but it has been observed that you can be successful in most of the cases where you weigh options very carefully based on the positive points and take calculated risks and therefore the number of such successes far outnumber the number of failures.
    28→
    29→Let me explain. Suppose out of 10 decisions to go ahead in an option after weighing and evaluating the risk coefficients and taking calculated risks, you may win over in 8 such cases but lose 2 out of them. It may be found that the profits and advantages that you receive from those 8 wins will far outnumber the losses and disadvantages you acquire out of the 2 losses. Therefore in net, you stand to gain in your decisions. But for a no risk taking leader, he may attempt only 2 options out of the 10 available and may be win none, therefore gaining nothing. That’s the difference between a positive minded risk taking leader and a negatively minded no risk taking leader.
    30→
    31→**The leader taking calculated risk always wins**.
    32→
    33→**The salient points are:**
    34→
    35→1. Always take calculated risks  
    36→2. Evaluate risk-probability matrix before deciding to take risks  
    37→3. Evaluate risk-returns matrix to evaluate profits and gains worth taking
    38→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “FOOLISH TARGET” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "888-906"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “FOOLISH TARGET” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Every CEO assigns a target to his colleagues for order booking. This target is often a SMURT, because though it is **S**pecific, **M**easurable, but unfortunately **U**nachievable, even if it is **R**esult driven and **T**ime bound. It is unlike a SMART target where the A (Achievable) is replaced by the U (Unachievable). SMURT basically defies one of the characters of a SMART goal. If you assign unachievable targets, what happens? The poor employee slogs out day and night to do his best, ignores his family and friends, most importantly ignores his health and sleep; develops stress often leading to medical problems and finally achieves slightly higher figures than otherwise it would have been but at the cost of relationship, health and a lifeless life, and yet does not achieve the set targets. It is like chasing the mirage in a desert. However near you try to go to a mirage, the further away you are from it. Are you aware of as to how many young professionals today are subjected to stress related heart problems often leading to heart attacks and cardiac arrest. Who is at fault? The company does not take any responsibility other than to facilitate medical insurance. But what about the permanent damage in one’s health?
    11→
    12→Let me put a word of caution for argument. Someone may argue that companies must give stiff targets to perform well. Yes, it’s right. But there is a difference between stiff targets and unachievable targets. Stiff targets are those which are practically possible with lot of hard work and efforts. They are difficult to achieve, nevertheless achievable. But unachievable targets are those which are impractical in spite of doing hard work with tremendous efforts. Sometimes management thinks that employees will work hard only if unachievable targets are given. But I am sorry to infer that in such cases you are probably fooling around or deceiving your colleagues. In other words any matured employee will definitely work hard even if stiff targets are given, then why unnecessarily give them unachievable targets only to demoralize and ruin their lives? Are they not aware of your intentions?
    13→
    14→The following example is interesting. Say, the market for an industry is USD 2500 Million. The Head knows, through his market intelligence, the likely targets given by its competitors to their respective Heads. The numbers are also approximated by studying the past financial reports of the competitors and what they are likely to target or achieve in a year. Then you astonishingly find that the summation of all targets of all the competitors as per your own estimate is a whopping USD 5000 Million. This means there is not enough market in line with the cumulative targets of different companies in the fray. It’s not that the Heads are unaware of what reasonable targets should be, given the competition, but it is a harsh and cruel technique adopted by senior leaders to overstate targets and dumped upon employees so that they run mad to achieve such impossible targets like the mirage in a desert. The Head knows that the employee, in this mad rush, may achieve slightly more than what would have been a reasonable stiff target. And therefore this additional achievement is the extra benefit that the Head has under his belt, albeit at the cost of ruining the lives of employees. Therefore the question comes of a work-life balance which I shall discuss later.
    15→
    16→While the organisation may gain temporarily with such unethical strategies, in the long run, a talented employee may resign or succumb to serious medical illness when continuously subjected to such impractical and unachievable targets. Sometimes, an employee, realising this syndrome, goes casual and is wise enough not to get mad and instead go easy, with a philosophy of “ke sera sera”, i.e. whatever will be, will be, because he knows at the beginning itself that these targets are SMURT or UNSMART targets, targets which are NOT achievable. Ultimately, the organisation loses to such smart employees subjected to SMURT goals.
    17→
    18→The targets set out by the Heads must be really SMART. Smart employees will accept SMART goals which may be difficult but achievable. Also HR should be vigilant to see whether employees have been engaged in too high stressed environments in a pursuit to achieve their targets. They should be identified and their cases discussed with the Heads. Employees are organization’s biggest asset. They must be taken care of and looked after by the HR. **Driving them mad in a quest to achieve the unachievable targets is a leader’s characteristic madness itself.** 
    19→
    20→**The salient points are:**
    21→
    22→1. Provide SMART goals which are difficult but are practically achievable  
    23→2. Smart employees accept SMART goals  
    24→3. Providing unachievable targets is a characteristic madness of a leader  
    25→4. Unachievable targets may force employees to run mad and destroy their health, families and relationships
    26→

     2→title: "THE “SECRET RELATION” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "907-934"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “SECRET RELATION” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Employees try to keep good relations with their seniors and Heads. However, the Heads must differentiate respect and good corporate relationship from oiling & flattering or even personal relationships. However good a person is in his behavior or performance, oiling and flattering must be wholly discouraged. The Head must percolate that while showing respect is a good gesture, oiling and flattering the Head is not at all desirable.
    11→
    12→Personal and Corporate relationships should be treated separately. This is actually very difficult practically. While an employee may be friendly, respectful and helpful to a Head and his family or be a true friend of his boss, the Head, at the same time, must ensure and indicate that the same person may get a bad rating in the corporate if his performance is poor. Performance and personal relation must be viewed separately. This attitude is not followed by many senior leaders because they succumb to the kindness and relation of the individual towards him and his family and more so, if the Head accepts oiling and flattering of the individual.
    13→
    14→The Head must make it clear to all employees, particularly to his close ones who are close to his family as friends, that he shall be unbiased and not mix personal relation with performance which shall be evaluated separately. Once the Head makes this clear right at the beginning, I believe that there will be no conflict between personal and corporate relationship. Employees need to acknowledge this but it depends on how the Head has communicated the same to his employees’ right at the beginning. Giving better scores in performance to an individual because of close personal relationship is a common syndrome existing in organisations. But I have seen Heads who are very professional and appraise individuals without bias even if some of them are close friends.
    15→
    16→Such biased performance appraisals also results in a commotion amongst deserving and honest employees because they are no fools not to know about these secret personal relationships. Performers, who are not friends of the boss or do not oil or flatter his boss, will tend to believe that reward is not definite for good performance but oiling and flattering surely is. This gives a very bad notion amongst the employees in an organization. Even trust between employees who takes advantage of their personal relationship with the boss and other employees fall apart.
    17→
    18→Therefore, since appraisals are very sensitive and important, the following may be observed.
    19→
    20→1. All Heads must convey to his employees that he shall be unbiased and will treat personal and corporate relations separately. This will develop mutual trust amongst employees.  
    21→     
    22→2. Promotion, increment and bonus must be strictly associated ONLY with the potential and performance of an employee irrespective of personal friendship of the employees with their bosses.   
    23→     
    24→3. Any unethical corporate activity by an individual shall be dealt as per the company rules irrespective of personal relationship.  
    25→     
    26→4. There is absolutely no harm if a Head develops friendship with his subordinate so long his corporate actions are unbiased irrespective of such personal relationships.  
    27→   
    28→
    29→**The salient points are:**
    30→
    31→1. Personal and corporate relationships must be treated separately  
    32→2. Personal relationships should not influence corporate appraisals  
    33→3. Distinction between personal and corporate relationships and treating them appropriately by leaders will generate trust amongst employees  
    34→4. Performance, potential, merit, talent and experience should be the only criteria for appraisals, personal relationship should find no place
    35→

     2→title: "THE “POSH” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "935-957"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “POSH” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→There are some leaders who seek sexual favours from female employees in lieu of promotion and/or increment and/or good bonus. These leaders assume that such employees will usually not have the courage to complain because of her position or because of accepting the favours. This happens in all kinds of organisations, and you can sometimes find reports of such incidents in the newspapers.
    11→
    12→Such intentions fall under the POSH (Prevention of Sexual Harassment) policy of an organization. Such intent of a leader must be strongly opposed by the superior leader as and when some lead is known. The concerned employee can also raise a complaint to the HR. She can as well discuss the matter with her friends and appeal for action from the HR Head. Such intention, if and when noticed by any other employee, junior or senior, can be reported without fear to HR Head. HR must provide a platform so that any employee can raise a concern regarding POSH. 
    13→
    14→Upon confirmed conviction, such leaders should be sacked with due notice, irrespective of how important he is to the organisation. Leaders must treat all female employees with respect. HR Head must roll out a “fearless” policy for employees so that they can report such cases without any fear. Seniors often go scot free because juniors feel that they may be victimised. The same feeling may occur with a female employee who may succumb to such unethical and disrespectful pressure on them. Leaders must give due respect to women employees and their talent and work and not take advantage just because the power to provide promotion/increment lies with him.
    15→
    16→A major dilemma that crops up is if the accused is a high performer in the organization. Sometimes it may so happen that the senior management is reluctant to take action immediately for fear of losing a high potential employee. But here is the question of principle of the organization. A good organization must adhere to its principles always to keep its reputation at the highest level, even if it understands that it will lose in some way when actions are taken against senior performing leaders.
    17→
    18→Today, women have shown great talent in every field of sphere. They have proved to the world that they are no less than men in achieving anything on this earth. From the best singers and politicians of all times to women having climbed the Mount Everest, flying big aircrafts, becoming great entrepreneurs and leaders of organisations, winning the ICC Cricket World Cup, becoming reputed scientists, academicians and teachers, becoming great fashion designers, directing great movies, women have printed their names in all spheres of life. They must be respected for their work and talent. Therefore they cannot be exploited by leaders by providing undue favours.
    19→
    20→If a female employee is not given justice even by their top most leaders, they can always report the matter to their group chairman and also lodge a police case and flash their stories in the social media. Leaders violating POSH policy must be taken to task. It is better for the HR Head to take a note of this before it is too late to react or defend. 
    21→
    22→POSH policy must be adhered to at all costs, even if it means sacking top performing leaders. Every employee including senior leaders need to sign the POSH policy before being recruited.
    23→
    24→**The salient points are:**
    25→
    26→1. POSH policy must be strictly adhered to  
    27→2. Stern action must be taken for POSH defaulters irrespective of their performance and potential in the organization  
    28→3. All women deserve respect, their talent are no less than men  
    29→4. Women must have different forums to complain on POSH matters
    30→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “FALSE INNOVATION” Syndrome"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "958-981"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “FALSE INNOVATION” Syndrome**
     9→
    10→Some Leaders sometimes cite about new innovative ideas which are actually copied from the internet or elsewhere. They know that these topics are in the nascent stage of development and not articulated in the newspapers or magazines. These leaders therefore take the advantage of his own employees being ignorant on the subject and thereby think that all his employees will digest such innovative information without a doubt and will chant “Sir, you are a great thinker”. Also the leader remains confident that even if someone knew that the topic being spoken is not new and is being researched currently, his employees will still remain quiet and not oppose out of fear of being victimized later on.
    11→
    12→For example, one leader said in an organization, “why not robotic soldiers be produced which can act as soldiers in the treacherous mountains”. Many were impressed, what a thought\! Brilliant\! People thought their leader really have great innovative ideas. They put the leader on an extremely high pedestal. However, they were ignorant that even India is thinking of the same. The leader just copied the thought from a news item.
    13→
    14→A senior employee thereafter researched on the internet and found that development of such robotic soldiers is on in India. He understood that his leader has copied the thought from the internet and conveyed that the thought was of his own.  
    15→   
    16→Today you cannot fool your employees because of Google or other search engines. I call it the ‘Google baba’.  And with AI powered search engines today, anything and everything can be found by Google baba. A leader may temporarily fool his employees by citing a “so called innovative idea” but sooner or later employees will find out the truth whether the innovation the leader is talking is really his or not\! 
    17→
    18→The employees may not point out such false “innovative ideas” to the leader lest he be victimized later. But eventually people form a bad opinion about such leaders who are lying or ignorant about a subject. The leader may not be able to know about the bad opinion formed in the minds of his employees until someone reveals it to him. Also, these bad opinions flare up as jokes and spread to all quarter of employees with laughter behind the scenes that the leader may not have knowledge of. 
    19→
    20→Honesty is a precious characteristic of a leader. It is better not to be innovative; if at all someone cannot than to lie on something which will eventually become a joke or a talking point everywhere outside the premises of your office. 
    21→
    22→Leaders must say things with caution. You must say what you know of, else you will be a part of the “False Innovation” syndrome that this chapter is talking about. You have seen occasionally how wrongly used words or lies stated by politicians flare up debates and fighting. As a leader, you must always talk and communicate honestly and be careful to what you say and what words or statements you use.
    23→
    24→Every leader can conduct a forum to listen to innovative ideas by their employees as well as provide his innovative ideas, may be once in every quarter, so that such innovation which makes sense can be practically implemented in their organization. The ideas must be checked on the internet to determine its originality. If the idea exists, the thought may not be innovative; nevertheless you can use the existing idea with modifications if necessary rather than reinventing the wheel. A leader should be innovative but at the same time also listen to innovative ideas of others. It is sometimes said that the Almighty have given us two ears and one mouth because we need to listen twice as much as we need to talk\!
    25→
    26→**The salient points are:**
    27→
    28→1. Leaders today must be careful in stating their ideas. False statements or lies may bring disrepute or could be a matter of joke.   
    29→2. Today, Google baba and AI can find all information for you. You cannot fool people so easily  
    30→3. Create a forum to discuss innovative ideas to implement.
    31→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “MISCOMMUNICATION” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "982-1004"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “MISCOMMUNICATION” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Today, employees are scattered at many offices and project sites of a company. All are not preview to what’s happening in the company or in its Head Office. Officers stationed in the Head Office know about certain things developing across the company. Similarly officers stationed at sites too may know of some secrets that the Head Office is ignorant of. If such information travelling across offices is correct or semi correct, then employees of the company should be communicated about the same on time so that rumors are not spread. If rampant rumors are spread across sites and offices, it becomes a grape vine which may be difficult to negate if acted too late.
    11→
    12→Once it so happened that rumors started spreading about a particular Business Unit being closed because of its poor financials. From my information network, I understood that some site employees belonging to the Business Unit were afraid and had started giving interviews to competitor companies. These employees were worried about their future. Rumors started picking up and spreading. The morale of the employees was diving south which started affecting the seriousness and quality of their work. Before it did any further damage, a communication was made to all the employees regarding the rumor which was finally put to rest. But by that time, the company had already lost some of its good performing employees.
    13→
    14→Thus lays the importance of communication. Performers today are very sensitive. They will take necessary and immediate action once they get some inkling of something undesirable that may fall upon them. As Head of Business Unit, I always articulated messages promptly on a common messaging forum once a month to all employees across the company on what important activities are happening in the company, the achievements of the month and the future endeavors of the company. Such communication provides a sense of belongingness amongst the employees and provides an environment where they can work with pride and happiness. With time, they were trusting such monthly communication instead of listening to scrap news floating here and there. They could boast about their company in the outside world as to how the company is gearing up for the future. The families too felt secured, proud and happy.
    15→
    16→I had also created an online suggestions platform where employees could put in their valuable suggestions. Every month, a core group would review the suggestions and consider those worth implementing. A company can also reward the top three suggestions and declare them in the forum if it had made to the implementation list. This makes employees to think that they are important because their suggestions, if found reasonable, are being implemented for the betterment of the company. HR must sensitise the importance of providing suggestions. HR must also indicate that the Management would like to implement those which will be found to be useful in the organization. In fact, once the suggestions are implemented, the same should be notified. A leader can make a suggestion-implementation matrix, where the suggestion would be specified with the name of the employee who provided the suggestion, the date of suggestion, the implementation date and a brief remark on how the company gained by implementing the suggestion. Such employees could be rewarded. 
    17→
    18→People have their eyes and ears opened everywhere in office and at different sites and are preview to many critical information that the Leaders may sometimes not be aware of. Such information could be true or simply rumors. However, their information may be extremely important to strategically position their company against stiff competition or reducing costs in company. Leaders must discuss the issues with their subordinates and communicate their critical thoughts on the matter to all its employees promptly.
    19→
    20→Once an employee happened to go by the side of a competitor’s project site and came across new construction equipment being used in the site for their foundation works. Immediately, upon due diligence, it was found that the equipment was normally used to speed up voluminous foundation works. The matter was immediately taken up at the Head Office. The HO after going through the details planned to purchase the equipment for its major sites. The same was communicated to all the employees through its communication channels. This is how the company benefited in speeding up its works.
    21→
    22→Proper communication in appropriate times is an attribute of great leadership. It can provide correct information, ideas, get suggestions and provide clarity, so that there is no anomaly or confusion in the minds of employees. Grape vine has no place in an organization. Communication must arrest rumours, mis-information and grape vines floating around an organization.
    23→
    24→**The salient points are:**
    25→
    26→1. Communication is an art to be mastered by all leaders  
    27→2. Proper communication can arrest rumours and grape vine  
    28→3. Communication provides clarity to employees and eradicates confusion  
    29→4. Communication is a medium to share good ideas across organisation
    30→

     2→title: "THE “MISUSE” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1

     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “MISUSE” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Some Leaders are habitually unethical and irresponsible spenders of money of the company. 
    11→
    12→Once a new ED was recruited in an organisation. Several EDs and Directors in the past worked successfully in the existing office but this ED thought that his office needs a grand makeover. The building also housed hundreds of employees of different levels. Upon proposal and convincing the Board, a hefty sum was approved and a complete renovation was carried out. The exchequer was quite high. After a few months, the President thought that the company’s Head Office should have been in another big city, incidentally where his home town was located. This too was approved by the Board and a new office was taken where all the top leaders of the company were relocated. The earlier President and the newly renovated ED’s office remained vacant as a waste while the other employees continued working in the same building in other floors as they were earlier. 
    13→
    14→Several questions come to one’s mind. Was such renovation of the ED’s existing office required? Was relocation of Top Management Office to a swanky office elsewhere required? Why was not this relocation of office thought before so that the renovation of ED’s office could have been avoided? Why, after a short time after renovation did thought come across to relocate to a bigger city? Are these expenses worthwhile with respect to the financials of the company? Did the earlier ED or Directors face so much of problems of space or inconvenience that they could not work properly? Could such renovation or part relocation wait for some more time? Can the relocated top leaders have meaningful coordination with the existing office now where most of their employees are still housed? Did not the Board think of all these issues while they provided their approval? Isn’t it therefore that such a renovation or relocation was a waste of company’s money?
    15→
    16→In another instance, customer’s representative comes to your office at around 11 am for a meeting. The leader craves for a good lunch. It’s quiet sometime that he did not savour a good buffet lunch in a five star hotel\! So, what does the Leader do? He unnecessarily hosts a lunch for the guest in a five star hotel after the meeting just to satisfy himself for the lunch even if the guests were not keen for the lunch. Very cunningly, the leader takes along with him a close subordinate for the lunch so that the subordinate signs off the bill (the leader being not the spender\!) who then sends the bill to the same leader for approval. The leader signs the bill. Alas\! Rs 25,000 gone from the coffers of the company simply for the fact that the leader actually wanted a good lunch, not the guests\!  
    17→
    18→Sometimes, to maintain cordial relationship, hosting a lunch or a dinner for a stakeholder is nothing wrong, but to do it numerous times to satisfy your own greed for good food at the cost of the company is unethical and waste of company’s money.
    19→
    20→Accounts Department needs to be vigilant on such avoidable expenses which are actually personal in nature but made official like I mentioned above. 
    21→
    22→Once there was a marriage of a Director’s daughter of a big client. The Director extended invitation to the Leader personally and not to others in the company. Hence this was purely a personal invitation. However, the Leader convinces his Management and Accounts that the invitation is to the company and therefore a gift needs to be presented on behalf of the company. The Management, upon trust, assumes that the invitation has been extended to some top leaders of the SBU and thereby approves the cost for an expensive gift. However, when the gift is handed over, it is done in a manner as if the gift has been presented by the leader in his personal capacity. 
    23→
    24→In all such cases where actually the leader was supposed to spend money ethically from his own pocket, he disguises every time as if the incidents are related to the company and thereafter spends the money from the coffers of the company. A leader is supposedly paid well; still he hankers after company’s money for personal expenses. It is an irony that such well to do people in a company are hungry to grab more and more facilities unethically and make or save more money irrespective of the wealth they may have already amassed. 
    25→
    26→Whether you want to make or save money, that’s entirely up to you, but definitely you cannot trick a company to pay for your personal events from their coffers\!  
    27→
    28→**The salient points are:**
    29→
    30→1. Expenses of a company must be optimized and regulated  
    31→2. Leaders must refrain from spending company’s expenses for their own vested interests  
    32→3. HR and Accounts must be vigilant to stop unnecessary spending from the company’s coffers by dishonest leaders
    33→

     2→title: "THE “BEST PROJECT AWARD” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "1031-1072"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “BEST PROJECT AWARD” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→The best executed project at site is the most deserving project for an award for a leader, but unfortunately the leader makes mistakes in such determination. Site execution cannot be the only criteria to be judged as the best executed project without taking cognisance of how and at what costs the project was financially closed. Therefore a Project should wait for its financial closure to be determined for award because rework during warranty period might reduce the profitability as determined after the physical completion of the project. Projects competing for awards must be financially closed first. Projects physically completed and commissioned but not financially closed should not be considered for award.
    11→
    12→Many management students in B-schools are not aware of what are all the terms of a financial closure of a project. Briefly, the same are as follows.
    13→
    14→1\. Physical completion of the project  
    15→2\. Commissioning of the project, i.e. say for an electric transmission project, the successful commencement of transmission of electricity  
    16→3\. Complete reconciliation of all billings billed to the client for the project and the payments received by the contractor  
    17→4\. Providing Retention Bank Guarantee  
    18→5\. Replacements and rework, if necessary, during warranty period at contractor’s cost  
    19→6\. Successful completion of warranty period.  
    20→7\. Retrieval of all bank guarantees from the client.
    21→
    22→As illustrated in a later chapter on the Project Health Card, the project parameters to be considered for the best project award are as follows:
    23→
    24→1\. Construction Time  
    25→2\. Direct & Indirect costs, overheads, finance costs, Total costs w.r.t. budget  
    26→3\. Profitability w.r.t Construction Time after considering rework and replacements during warranty period  
    27→4\. Use of Technology  
    28→5\. Quality of construction  
    29→6\. Safety methods implemented during construction  
    30→7\. Ethical standards  
    31→8\. Sustainability implementation  
    32→9\. CSR project undertaken   
    33→10\. Cash Flow of the project  
    34→11\. Financial closure of project
    35→
    36→The SBU Head should provide a weightage to each of the above parameters for each project closed financially and thereafter judge which project is the best FINANCIALLY closed project. Normally the leader tend to judge the best project based on the top three parameters as illustrated above, which is wrong.
    37→
    38→Use of Technology ensures better quality of work. Quality of construction ensures longer durability of the project. Safety methods ensure safety and wellbeing of human lives. Ethical standards raise the name and fame of an organisation. Sustainability measures indicate caring for your environment and the future of our earth. Cash Flow determines the requirement of capital for the project. And financial closure frees the organisation from all financial obligations of the project. Hence the other eight parameters too deserve significant weightage.
    39→
    40→Suppose a project calls for huge cash capital for execution of the project but yield average profits. The capital requirement of the project may be so big that it may have disturbed the cash flow and timely completion of other projects. Then the question is whether such an order was worth for the company? Can such a financially closed project involving huge capital and yielding average profits be considered for award even if it was executed well? Thus determining the best project for award is a critical judgment based on all the parameters discussed and their associated weightages.
    41→
    42→Often the leaders consider a particular project as the best without deep diving on all the parameters of a good executed and financially closed project. Best executed project is also sometimes chosen based on the leader’s relationship with the Project Manager. Such malpractices will dissolve if there is a fair mechanism to judge the best project.
    43→
    44→**The salient points are:**
    45→
    46→1. The best financially closed project is the best executed project  
    47→2. Various parameters of Financial closure must be taken into account for evaluating the best project   
    48→3. Award must not be given to best physically completed project but given to best financially closed project which also considers physical completion
    49→

     2→title: "THE “BID LOSS BLAME” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "1073-1098"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “BID LOSS BLAME” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→In a bid, various components of costs are considered by the Bidding team of an organisation. The Design Head provides design data based on which costs are evaluated. The Supply Chain Head provides best quotes of vendors. The Finance Head provides the best financing and bank guarantee costs. The Project Management Head provides the best Site management and overhead costs. The HR Head provides the salaries of different grades of Project and Site manpower. The Advertisement Head provides the annual advertising costs to be appropriated and considered in each bid. The Equipment Management Head provides the cost of using equipment and machinery for a project, and so on and so forth.
    11→
    12→The Bidding Head is responsible to collate all information, documents and costs from various departments and compile the price bid to be submitted to the client. So far as costs are considered, he discusses and negotiates with all departmental Heads to get the best competitive costs to be incorporated in the bid. Of course the Bidding Head also considers the market, competition, likely quote of competitors, importance of winning the project to determine the profit levels to be considered before finalizing the price of the bid.
    13→
    14→Some costs mentioned above have a major impact in the bid cost, and some costs have low impact. For example, the vendor costs have a major impact in the bid cost since supplies may take up 70% of the total bid cost.
    15→
    16→People should understand that Bidding & Marketing department provides only the Marketing costs of the company to be incorporated as overhead costs in the bid. All other costs are from other different Departmental Heads. The main job of the Bidding team and the Head is to put a proper documentation of all techno-commercial requirements as required and specified in the bidding documents (Part – 1: Techno-commercial bid) and the Price Schedules of different Supplies and Services required for the Project (Part – 2: Price bid)
    17→
    18→Now here is the problem. Whenever a bid is lost, the Director often pounces on the Bidding Head as if he was the main culprit for losing the bid. All other Departmental Heads like the Finance Head, HR Head, SCM Head, etc. are scot free and takes no accountability for the loss even if the quotes given by them during bid submission were actually not competitive. It is shameful that the Bidding Head is often the scape goat and the poor chap has to bear the brunt of bid losses. Such a thought process needs to change in the industry. In fact, I will discuss in more details in a subsequent chapter, that one of the evaluation criteria in the Annual Appraisals **of all Departments** of an organisation that must be incorporated is the percentage of bid win. The weightage of such criteria across different Departments may vary depending on the cost impact of the Departmental cost towards bid cost. Unfortunately, the weightage for percentage of bids won in all other Departments is not considered as a KRA in their Appraisals, as if no other Department other than the Marketing & Bidding Department was responsible for bid losses. This is a very unfortunate phenomenon existing in organisations. Various Departments must be responsible for bid losses to the extent of their cost impact towards the bid. Similarly, they must be rewarded for bid wins to the extent of their cost impacts. It is also not correct that the bidding team takes full credit of bid wins when other departments contributed to the bid cost leading to winning bids.
    19→
    20→Particularly, the major costs of a bid are the vendors which may take up 65% to 80% of total bid cost and the Project management costs at site which may take up 20% to 30% of the bid cost. Hence the SCM Head responsible for vendor costs and the PM Head responsible for Project Management costs are the major cost impact departments. Other departments may impact around 10% of bid costs which cannot be ignored because even a non-competitive cost offer from this 10% impact zone may lead to a bid loss by a thin margin. 
    21→
    22→Also there is a question of a bad win and a good win. This aspect will be discussed in a separate chapter.
    23→
    24→Therefore, when a bid is lost, a Director may question not only the Bidding Head but also the SCM Head and the PM or Operations Head. The Bidding Head cannot be the only scape goat. 
    25→
    26→**The salient points are:**
    27→
    28→1. A Price bid has to consider costs given by different Departments  
    29→2. The SCM and the Project Management Departments are the bulk of the costs in a Price bid  
    30→3. The Bidding Department develops only the Marketing Costs on their own, all other costs are provided by other Departments  
    31→4. The Bidding Head alone cannot be responsible for bid wins and losses  
    32→5. Appraisals must provide appropriate weightage to all Departments for bid wins and losses based on their cost impact in the Price bid
    33→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “WILD DREAMS” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "1099-1118"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “WILD DREAMS” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→I always say to people “Don’t be nonsensically optimistic or be unnecessarily pessimistic, be logically realistic”. We always should have optimism. That’s the way it should be. When a glass has half the water, optimism is that the glass is half full whereas pessimism is that the glass is half empty. Both the phrases are theoretically correct, but it is the way you look at things. You should not tell your child that you will fail if you do not study, rather always tell that you shall score well if you study well. Again, both the phrases mean the same; it is only how you convey. Positive conveying has lot of positive impact on the minds of people. Such positivity creates enthusiasm, oneness and belongingness which eventually lead to better results.
    11→
    12→Now here is the issue at hand. Should you dream and think positive without creating any infrastructure for the same? A child can say that he wants to become first in class but he needs the academic support (the books, stationary, coaching, etc.), the attitude to study and the talent to understand the topics to become first in the class. Isn’t it? You may dream to go to the moon, that’s fine, but for that you need to train yourself so that you can tolerate the environment and the gravitational issue while at the moon as well as set up an infrastructure for your training to travel to the moon. In other words, people can dream of any practically achievable goal, but one needs to prepare for the same. 
    13→
    14→**Infrastructure support, attitude and talent** are the three mandatory keys to succeed in your goals. Also, depending on the particular level of the goal, the infrastructure support, attitude and talent has to be adequate and commensurate. For example, to become first in the IIT examinations, you may need a support for several books and coaching classes, develop attitude to study well and pass mock tests and also must have the talent to understand the difficult topics well to become first in the examinations. Isn’t it?
    15→
    16→Why I am saying all these when everyone knows about the same. Here’s why. Once, a leader announced that just in three years’ time the company’s revenues must cross one of its stiff competitors. This competitor was at number one at that time with revenues five times more than that of the company. Accordingly, overnight the annual targets and CAGR were modified to achieve the estimated revenues of the competitor in the next three years. Beautiful coloured graphs were drawn displaying revenues of the competitor being overtaken in a period of three years. This exercise was only pure mathematics. While all the employees were ready to endeavour and had the talent to achieve the same, the infrastructure support was missing. For example, such a large growth required a robust bidding team to acquire more projects, enhance the equipment and machinery capability, strengthen sources of finance, increase capital base, increase manpower in every Department to facilitate the envisaged growth, etc. But everything was missing and had no plans in the near future. The envisaged growth was probably just a wish.
    17→
    18→In another instance, there were some international projects which were to be bid on firm costs. The bid involved costs of several raw materials like Aluminum, Zinc and steel whose costs were fluctuating over the years. Also the bid was to be quoted in Dollars but some Indian vendors were quoting in Indian Rupees and were reluctant to quote in Dollars. So there came in the risk of currency fluctuation too. To de-risk from all these material cost fluctuations and currency fluctuations, the company needed to hedge the costs. But the Finance department had no idea at all on the process of hedging and therefore was also very shaky to undergo the process. The point I am making is while the company had tall dreams to acquire international projects, it did not have the knowledge or infrastructure to undertake hedging process with the banks.   
    19→
    20→It is good to be optimistic and dream positively, but such dreams must be realistic and logical and the infrastructure support, attitude and talent have to be positioned accordingly. Otherwise, this is what I call “Wild Dreams”, dreams to remain as dreams, not to be achieved.
    21→
    22→**The salient points are:**
    23→
    24→1. Don’t be nonsensically optimistic or be unnecessarily pessimistic, be logically realistic  
    25→2. Infrastructure support, attitude and talent are the three mandatory keys to succeed in your goals  
    26→3. Dream big, but create your support, attitude and capability to materialize your dreams. Without these, dreams remain as dreams.
    27→

     2→title: "THE “FOREIGN TOUR” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "1119-1151"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE “FOREIGN TOUR” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→In many organizations, there is a craze amongst senior eligible employees to go abroad for official work. While such official works are necessary for the growth of an organization, their other interests are also to save some time and visit tourist spots and also save (earn) money from the daily dollar/euro allowance that he gets for each day. 
    11→
    12→There is no harm in touring abroad for business opportunities or for preparation of future bids. But such visits cannot go on and on at the cost of company’s expenses without any significant results in some period of time. I agree that there is a gestation period for any new business to take off; you simply cannot expect a business to come from a new market after one visit to the country, but at the same time there is always a limit for that. You cannot go abroad dozens of times and yet significant results don’t come.
    13→
    14→I have come across such senior people who have gone to the same country again and again to seek opportunity in a new domain of business, even with the support of his Director, but without any significant progress even after multiple visits. You can of course go multiple times to a same country but the progress of each visit must be appreciable. A company spends lot of money for a foreign travel. You cannot take a company to task by going several times and yet not produce any appreciable progress. 
    15→
    16→The best method to keep a track of the progress of foreign visits is to maintain a register noting the travel details, duration, money spent, meetings held and progress till date. Minutes of the Tour (MoT) detailing discussions held in the meetings and forthcoming actions needs to be recorded. The register should also set a budget for the marketing expenses and the cost of visits to foreign countries and the expectation of returns in terms of obtaining business. If these expenses are very high with respect to benefits achieved or benefits estimated to receive in future, then such marketing in the country under question may be abandoned. 
    17→
    18→A cost-returns matrix must be made to analyse probabilities of obtaining business. Returns must be multiple times of the cost. While getting business, the revenues cannot be considered as returns, rather profits earned from the business should be considered as returns because profits are the actual amounts earned by a company, not the revenues. The returns may be low in the beginning but with each passing year the returns must be more before the business gets saturated at a future point of time.  
    19→For example, assume that the cost of visits, marketing and setting up an office in a foreign country is Rs 3 Crores in a year. A leader, say, spends two years into marketing in the country before he starts getting a business. After two years, say, the organization gets two orders of Rs 100 Cr each, both with a completion period of 2 years. Assume that each project is completed by 50% each year and the projects make a margin of 5%. Then here is the Marketing cost-returns matrix (All figures are in Rs Cr)
    20→
    21→Year	Opening	Cost		Orders  	Executed 	Profit		Closing  
    22→	Balance	incurred	Received	Revenues	Margin	Balance  
    23→1	0		3		0		0		0		0  
    24→2	0		3		0		0		0		0  
    25→3	0		3		200		100		5		100  
    26→4	100		3		200		150		7.5		150  
    27→5	150		3		200		175		8.75		175  
    28→6	175		3		200		187.5		9.375		187.5  
    29→Total after 6 years:	18		800		612.5		30.625
    30→
    31→Above was a simple exercise. I have not considered the time value of money to make it simple to understand. You can observe that after 6 years, while you have incurred a cumulative cost of Rs 18 Cr, you have earned profits of Rs 30.625 Cr. But for this, you need a continuous flow of orders and a continuous executing to earn the profits.  
    32→    
    33→Future business is all about estimates from your experience. You have to be rational in your expectations considering the business potential available in the country, the competition existing, the strategy you employ to secure business, etc. If your expectations were as above, it makes sense to continue marketing efforts for the next couple of years as you can find from the Cost-Returns matrix though the gestation period in the above example was 2 years, in other words, there was no earnings in the first two years but the expenses had to be incurred.  
    34→
    35→**The salient points are:**
    36→
    37→1. Evaluate MoTs after every foreign tour to determine progress made  
    38→2. Abandon or continue foreign tours based on progressive results and future estimated returns  
    39→3. Determine Marketing-cost matrix to understand worthwhileness of your efforts
    40→

     2→title: "THE “FORCE YOUR STYLE” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "1152-1168"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**THE “FORCE YOUR STYLE” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Leaders, irrespective of whether you are the MD or a Director or any CXO or a SBU Head or a BU Head or a Departmental Head, have different styles of leadership patterns. These patterns are generally developed by individual leaders while they were growing up the ladder to senior positions and eventually became top leaders.
    11→
    12→All leadership styles are different from each other, some may be effective for some, and some others may be effective for others. There are nothing called right or wrong kinds of leadership. All leadership styles have advantages and disadvantages and that is why some leaders tactfully employ different leadership styles at different times.
    13→
    14→Once there was a CXO who always insisted his style of work to his subordinate. The CXO thought that his kind of leadership style is the only effective style that everyone needs to adopt. The CXO did not appreciate the subordinate having a different style of effective leadership and did not think that forcing upon him another kind of leadership style will not help him to achieve desired results. A top leader must be interested in achieving the result irrespective of the leadership style that his subordinate follows so long the leadership style is effective, ethical and SMART. Bulldozing a leader’s style down below is a sign of being adamant and displaying dictatorship.
    15→
    16→One must develop his own style of leadership and the style which his boss or his leader adopts may not necessarily be copied. The leader must also be democratic allowing his subordinate to follow his own style of leadership so long the results are ethically effective. 
    17→
    18→I have not discussed here the different style of leadership in practice which you can get to read in any leadership book. This chapter is on how you expect others to practice leadership. Interestingly, long time back, I had come across an article in a newspaper narrating the Gandhian style and the Hitlarian style of leadership. I was impressed by its different styles and I used to follow in the corporate having found it very effective. These styles have been described in an Annexure later.  
    19→   
    20→**The salient points are:**
    21→
    22→1. Develop your own style of leadership as you feel right. Don’t copy styles of your leaders unnecessarily  
    23→2. You may adopt different styles to be effective in different circumstances
    24→

1→---
     2→title: "THE “DELIBERATE PROCASTINATION” SYNDROME"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "1169-1187"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**THE “DELIBERATE PROCASTINATION” SYNDROME**
     9→
    10→Once, one BU Head, who was reporting to his SBU Head, was given the responsibility of securing a negotiated project in Africa. The award of project required a competitive pricing as well as lot of documentation to prove the credibility and experience of the organization. 
    11→
    12→Meetings were held occasionally and documents had to be revised or modified in line with the client’s requirements from time to time. Sometimes there were new documents required to be submitted. The SBU Head was to retire in a few months from then. The SBU Head, during review meetings, enquired regarding the progress of the expected award and found that the progress was quite slow.
    13→
    14→Actually, the BU Head was processing the case slowly. He was not in a mood to speed up the award process, in which case the SBU Head would take the major credit for receiving the award before his retirement was due. The objective of the BU Head was to go slow in such a manner that the award would be received after the retirement of the SBU Head, in which case he would have the entire liberty to claim full credit for bagging the award.
    15→
    16→The above attitude is condemnable. Firstly, the SBU Head also had a role to play to receive the award based on his initial negotiations. The BU Head trying to discredit him by receiving the award intentionally after the SBU Head’s retirement is not right in its first place. Secondly, life teaches that opportunity does not come again and again. In case you intentionally delay to receive an award, you risk your organization by giving your competitor to sneak in and grab the opportunity in which case you may not be considered for the award. Thirdly, every organization has quarterly and annual targets to be achieved. By delaying receipt of awards, you are actually depriving the organization to achieve the quarterly or annual targets. Even if the annual targets were in place, the award received would have increased the annual orders leading to better increments and bonus to the employees.
    17→
    18→Therefore the SBU Head must be vigilant if any of his subordinates are intentionally delaying any process in the organization. For this, regular reviews should be in place and the problem of delay identified right at the beginning. If required, the responsibility can be shifted to some other honest individual who would try his best to speed up the process of an expected award. Deliberate procrastination must be dealt with severely.  
    19→
    20→**The salient points are:**
    21→
    22→1. Deliberate procrastination is highly condemned. It may rob the organization of opportunities  
    23→2. Intentional procrastination to credit oneself and discredit someone else who also deserves credit is a crime. Such leaders must be dealt with sternly  
    24→3. Regular reviews must be held to arrest deliberate procrastination. If required, the responsibility can be shifted to others
    25→

1→---
     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 1
     4→section: 1
     5→original_lines: "1188-1237"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Skip level meetings**  
    11→Often the level below yours do not get chance to voice their views to your bosses. In fact sometimes the reporting level tries to suppress the voices of their subordinates to their bosses for vested reasons. In a skip level meeting with a leader, the leader keeps his reporting level in abeyance and interacts with the employees’ one level below so that the leader is aware of important things that may have been ignored or kept secret by his reporting level. Understanding the business processes may be clearer during interaction in the skip level meetings. There could be good suggestions too coming up during these discussions which the reporting level might have ignored.
    12→
    13→**Democratic leadership**  
    14→In major decisions, take inputs from your team members. After all it is the team members who have to deliver. By considering their inputs, you raise their morale, motivation, level of engagement and confidence. Do not be autocratic for major decisions. 
    15→
    16→**Supportive delegation**  
    17→When you delegate jobs to people, make sure you provide them with the right support and guidance. Without support, your delegation will be an instruction without results. Proper support engages people well avoiding confusion and frustration.
    18→
    19→**Team building**  
    20→When you build a team with members having diverse capabilities, you succeed in your ventures. Individual brilliance seldom achieves corporate goals. Developing team creates a supportive work environment.
    21→
    22→**Monthly MIS review**  
    23→This will track progress in all aspects of business. It will give insights to areas of improvement. The problem areas can be identified and addressed during such meetings. The clients and vendors can be immediately informed about critical issues that the organization is facing. Different Heads can be roped in as required to boost the performance in projects. Each project should be reviewed in details including its financials and action points noted. The overheads may also be determined to keep a track of the same. Market share analysis must also be tracked every month including those of the competitors.
    24→
    25→**Quarterly revision of targets**  
    26→If need be, based on unknown factors earlier and the market conditions, the KRAs may be tweaked to make them reasonable and SMART. Goals which are SMART are accepted by employees with a challenge.  
    27→
    28→**Monthly meetings for work environment and organizational issues**  
    29→Apart from business results understood from MIS reviews, work related issues are identified in these meetings. There could be areas concerning software requirements, or modifying existing processes, or boosting the hardware of the existing IT systems, or restructuring ergonomics in the office space, etc. Solving work related issues leads to better job satisfaction amongst employees, optimizes time and provides a “feel good” factor, all leading to a better work culture in the organization.
    30→
    31→**Monthly recognition Certificates to the best performer**  
    32→Good monthly performance should be rewarded. This will create a healthy competition amongst employees in the future which will improve organizational performance as a whole. Also the morale of the winners will be boosted for better performance. Non winners can be more enthusiastic to try harder to get an award in the next month.
    33→
    34→**Unconventional thinking**  
    35→Support and appreciate unconventional thinking. Many new thoughts may not be found to be effective, but some may be exceptionally brilliant. Arouse the culture of unconventional thinking in the organisation. May be you have a quarterly session for the purpose. 
    36→
    37→**Find the right successor**  
    38→Depending on potential, you have to pick someone and train him as your successor. Leaders must ensure the future of an organization and hence its importance.
    39→
    40→**Promote knowledge and skills to employees through Training**  
    41→There is no end to learning in our lives. From a newly born baby till death, we learn so many things in our lives on a daily basis. Coming to corporates, similarly, training in different fields of expertise must be carried on to enhance the capabilities of employees. This will help employees improve and achieve desired goals of the organization.
    42→
    43→**Meeting each Department quarterly**  
    44→Each supporting Department has issues of their own. All such issues cannot be looked into in a monthly MIS meeting which is basically driven to review business results. Yet Departmental issues play a critical role in the performance of an organization. Hence looking into issues faced by each Department, at least once in a quarter, and resolving them is of significant importance.
    45→
    46→**Be unbiased towards all employees**  
    47→Treat all equally based on performance and potential, irrespective of sex, caste, creed, colour, religion and also importantly irrespective of your personal relationship. This will create a healthy environment in the organization. Employees will believe what is being said and done is right. It will create trust amongst employees and also towards the organization and foster healthy environment. If you are treated equally, there will be a tendency to accept if you are wrong at times. It builds up an atmosphere of true spirit in the organization. 
    48→
    49→**Communicate well**  
    50→Once every month, send a communication across the organization on all the developments that happened in the month and the future endeavours discussed. Employees will take pride to work in the organization knowing well about the developments and likely future. Proper communication will also arrest rumours and grape vine, if any, floating in and around the organization.  
    51→
    52→**Wish on birthdays**  
    53→Ask HR team to send your wishes for birthday of your employees. Birthday wishes brings happiness to employees, that too from senior leaders. This creates togetherness in an organization. Employees feel proud that their leaders remember their days of joy
    54→
    55→**Get Well Soon cards**  
    56→Ask HR to send Get Well Soon cards to employees suffering from an ailment and on leave. Employees would feel that they are cared for. They will always give their best performance to their leaders and the organization once they resume work.
    57→
